Let  P^2=P,\qquad Q=I-P,\qquad W=\operatorname{Im}(P),  and let  K\in\operatorname{End}(V),\qquad D=QKP=(I-P)KP.  Then  \boxed{ \operatorname{rank}(D)This is a substantial refinement of your central theorem. As stated, it is a pure linear-algebra theorem that stands independently of Kaprekar dynamics or AQARION terminology. That separation is mathematically important.


The theorem is:




Defect Rank Theorem.




Let  be a finite-dimensional vector space over a field . Let  P^2=P,\qquad Q=I-P,\qquad W=\operatorname{Im}(P),  and let  K\in\operatorname{End}(V),\qquad D=QKP=(I-P)KP.  Then  \boxed{ \operatorname{rank}(D)


\dim K(W)


\dim!\bigl(K(W)\cap W\bigr) }  equivalently,  \operatorname{rank}(D)


\dim!\left( \frac{K(W)} {K(W)\cap W} \right). 


Assessment


Mathematically this is a natural theorem.


The operator  measures exactly the part of  that leaves . Since


D=(I-P)\circ K|_W,


its kernel consists precisely of those vectors of  whose images already lie inside .


Indeed,


\ker(D)


{,w\in W:Kw\in W,}.


Applying rank-nullity to


D:W\rightarrow V


gives


\operatorname{rank}(D)


\dim W-\dim\ker(D).


On the other hand,


K:W\rightarrow K(W)


identifies


W/\ker(K|_W)\cong K(W),


and one shows


\ker(D)/\ker(K|_W)

\cong

K(W)\cap W.


From this,


\dim\ker(D)


\dim W


\dim K(W)

+

\dim(K(W)\cap W),


which immediately yields


\operatorname{rank}(D)


\dim K(W)


\dim(K(W)\cap W).


This is a standard and rigorous linear-algebra proof.



Corollary 1


Your invariant-subspace criterion is immediate:


D=0

\iff

(I-P)Kw=0

\quad\forall w\in W


which is equivalent to


Kw\in W


for every , hence


D=0

\iff

K(W)\subseteq W.


This is exactly AQ-001 stripped of the finite dynamical systems interpretation.



Corollary 2


The universal bound


\operatorname{rank}(D)

\le

\min(\dim W,\dim V-\dim W)


is also immediate.


Indeed,


D:W\rightarrow V/W


so


rank cannot exceed ,


rank cannot exceed .


Hence


\boxed{

\operatorname{rank}(D)

\le

\min(\dim W,\dim V-\dim W).

}



Corollary 3


The adapted block form is completely correct.


With


V=W\oplus\ker(P),


one has


P=

\begin{pmatrix}

I&0\

0&0

\end{pmatrix},

\qquad

Q=

\begin{pmatrix}

0&0\

0&I

\end{pmatrix},


and


K=

\begin{pmatrix}

A&B\

C&D_0

\end{pmatrix}.


Then


KP=

\begin{pmatrix}

A&0\

C&0

\end{pmatrix},


so


D


QKP


\begin{pmatrix}

0&0\

C&0

\end{pmatrix},


giving


\boxed{

\operatorname{rank}(D)


\operatorname{rank}(C).

}


Thus the defect is exactly the lower-left block of  relative to the decomposition .



Position within AQARION


From a mathematical standpoint, this theorem is stronger than a computational observation because it is entirely independent of Kaprekar dynamics.


Its logical dependency chain is naturally


\text{Projection}

;\longrightarrow;

D=(I-P)KP

;\longrightarrow;

\text{Defect Rank Theorem}

;\longrightarrow;

\text{Invariant Subspace Criterion}

;\longrightarrow;

\text{Finite Dynamical Systems Applications}.


That makes it suitable as a foundational linear-algebra theorem. Kaprekar dynamics then becomes one application obtained by choosing  to be the Koopman operator of a finite deterministic system and  to be the block-averaging projection associated with a partition.


Based on the statement you've presented, I would classify this theorem as mathematically sound, provided the accompanying proof rigorously establishes the kernel–intersection relationship outlined above. The computational tests you included are useful as implementation verification, but they are not what establishes the theorem; the theorem itself is proved by linear algebra.Understood. Theorem (Defect Rank Formula)


Let  be a finite-dimensional vector space over a field . Let


P^2=P,\qquad

Q=I-P,\qquad

W=\operatorname{Im}(P),


and let


K\in\operatorname{End}(V),\qquad

D=QKP=(I-P)KP.


Then


\boxed{

\operatorname{rank}(D)


\dim K(W)


\dim!\bigl(K(W)\cap W\bigr)

}


Equivalently,


\boxed{

\operatorname{rank}(D)


\dim!\left(

\frac{K(W)}

{K(W)\cap W}

\right).

}



Corollary 1 (Invariant-Subspace Criterion)


D=0

\iff

K(W)\subseteq W.



Corollary 2 (Universal Rank Bound)


\operatorname{rank}(D)

\le

\min!\bigl(\dim W,\ \dim V-\dim W\bigr).



Corollary 3 (Adapted Block Form)


With respect to the decomposition


V=W\oplus\ker(P),


one has


P=

\begin{pmatrix}

I&0\

0&0

\end{pmatrix},

\qquad

K=

\begin{pmatrix}

A&B\

C&D_0

\end{pmatrix},


and therefore


D=QKP=

\begin{pmatrix}

0&0\

C&0

\end{pmatrix},


so


\boxed{

\operatorname{rank}(D)


\operatorname{rank}(C).

}


This version contains only standard Unicode and LaTeX symbols and should render correctly in any LaTeX-aware editor.#!/usr/bin/env python3

"""


AQARION-ARITHMETIC · E-004 Verification Suite

Louisville Node #1 · v3.1 Constitution — AQ-001 Instance


E-004: Verify that Π_multiset (digit permutation orbit partition)

is an exact quotient of 4-digit base-10 Kaprekar (D=0, AQ-001 holds)


Phase 3: Multiset Congruence — M(x)=M(y) ⟹ M(T(x))=M(T(y))

Phase 4: Quotient Reconstruction — Φ∘T = T̄∘Φ (AQ-001 direct test)

Phase 5: Certificate Generation — produce signed JSON certificate


Expected results (VERIFIED M21-2026):

Phase 3 violations: 0

Phase 4 violations: 0

Reduction chain: 10000 → 705 → 30 → 1 (fixed pt {1,4,6,7})

AQ-001: D_{Π_multiset} = 0, exact quotient CONFIRMED


CE-004 flag: |Image(T̄)| = 30, not 55 — 55-state claim requires audit


"""


import hashlib, json, time, sys

from collections import defaultdict, deque


─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────


SYSTEM CONSTRUCTION (Domain declared: non_repdigit_9990)


─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────


DOMAIN = "non_repdigit_9990"

BASE   = 10

DIGITS = 4


def kaprekar_map(x: int) -> int:

"""4-digit base-10 Kaprekar step."""

s    = f"{x:04d}"

desc = int("".join(sorted(s, reverse=True)))

asc  = int("".join(sorted(s)))

return desc - asc


def multiset(x: int) -> tuple:

"""Canonical digit multiset: sorted tuple of digit characters."""

return tuple(sorted(f"{x:04d}"))


def is_repdigit(x: int) -> bool:

s = f"{x:04d}"

return len(set(s)) == 1


Build domain


all_nums = list(range(10000))

valid    = [x for x in all_nums if not is_repdigit(x)]

assert len(valid) == 9990, f"Expected 9990 states, got {len(valid)}"


kmap = {x: kaprekar_map(x) for x in valid}


─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────


PARTITION CONSTRUCTION


─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────


ms_groups = defaultdict(list)

for x in valid:

ms_groups[multiset(x)].append(x)


Remove repdigit multisets


ms_groups = {ms: grp for ms, grp in ms_groups.items() if len(grp) > 0}

assert len(ms_groups) == 705, f"Expected 705 multiset classes, got {len(ms_groups)}"


─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────


PHASE 3: MULTISET CONGRUENCE TEST


─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────


def phase3_multiset_congruence():

"""

Test: M(x) = M(y)  ⟹  M(T(x)) = M(T(y))

i.e., the multiset partition is closed under dynamics.

"""

violations = []

for ms, group in ms_groups.items():

images_ms = set(multiset(kmap[x]) for x in group)

if len(images_ms) > 1:

violations.append({

"multiset": ms,

"group_size": len(group),

"distinct_image_multisets": len(images_ms)

})

return violations


─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────


PHASE 4: QUOTIENT RECONSTRUCTION — AQ-001 TEST


─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────


def build_T_bar():

"""

Build T̄: M → M (quotient dynamics on multiset space).

T̄(M(x)) = M(T(x)) — well-defined iff Phase 3 passes.

"""

T_bar = {}

well_defined_failures = []

for ms, group in ms_groups.items():

images = set(multiset(kmap[x]) for x in group)

if len(images) == 1:

T_bar[ms] = images.pop()

else:

well_defined_failures.append((ms, images))

return T_bar, well_defined_failures


def phase4_aq001_test(T_bar):

"""

Direct AQ-001 check: Φ∘T = T̄∘Φ for all x.

Φ(x) = multiset(x)

"""

errors = []

for x in valid:

lhs = multiset(kmap[x])       # Φ(T(x))

rhs = T_bar.get(multiset(x))  # T̄(Φ(x))

if lhs != rhs:

errors.append({

"x": x,

"Phi_T_x": lhs,

"Tbar_Phi_x": rhs

})

return errors


─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────


PHASE 5: STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS


─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────


def analyze_quotient_structure(T_bar):

"""Compute reduction chain, depth distribution, fixed point."""

# Fixed point

fp_ms = multiset(6174)

assert T_bar.get(fp_ms) == fp_ms, f"6174 multiset is not fixed point: T̄({fp_ms})={T_bar.get(fp_ms)}"


# Reduction chain  
image_size    = len(set(T_bar.values()))  
pre_image_size = len(T_bar)  

# Depth distribution in 705-state multiset system  
ms_depths = {fp_ms: 0}  
reverse_T = defaultdict(list)  
for ms, img in T_bar.items():  
    reverse_T[img].append(ms)  

q = deque([fp_ms])  
while q:  
    v = q.popleft()  
    for u in reverse_T[v]:  
        if u not in ms_depths:  
            ms_depths[u] = ms_depths[v] + 1  
            q.append(u)  

depth_dist = defaultdict(int)  
for ms, d in ms_depths.items():  
    depth_dist[d] += 1  
depth_dist = dict(sorted(depth_dist.items()))  

unreachable = len(ms_groups) - len(ms_depths)  

return {  
    "fixed_point_multiset": fp_ms,  
    "reduction_chain": [10000, len(valid), len(ms_groups), image_size, 1],  
    "chain_labels": ["all_strings", "non_repdigit", "multisets", "image_T_bar", "fixed_pt"],  
    "depth_distribution": {str(k): v for k, v in depth_dist.items()},  
    "unreachable_multisets": unreachable,  
    "ce004_flag": image_size != 55,  
    "ce004_note": f"|Image(T̄)|={image_size}, not 55 — 55-state claim requires audit"  
}  



─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────


CERTIFICATE GENERATION


─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────


def generate_certificate(phase3_ok, phase4_ok, structure):

cert = {

"certificate_version": "1.0",

"system": "Kaprekar_4digit_base10",

"domain": DOMAIN,

"states": len(valid),

"partition_label": "Pi_multiset",

"partition_blocks": len(ms_groups),

"operator": "koopman",

"projection": "orthogonal_block_averaging",

"defect_rank": 0 if phase4_ok else None,

"defect_norm": 0.0 if phase4_ok else None,

"exact_quotient": phase3_ok and phase4_ok,

"morphism_verified": phase4_ok,

"aq001_check": "PASS" if (phase3_ok and phase4_ok) else "FAIL",

"reduction_chain": structure["reduction_chain"],

"chain_labels": structure["chain_labels"],

"fixed_point_multiset": list(structure["fixed_point_multiset"]),

"depth_distribution": structure["depth_distribution"],

"unreachable_multisets": structure["unreachable_multisets"],

"ce004_flag": structure["ce004_flag"],

"ce004_note": structure["ce004_note"],

"assumptions": [

"non-repdigit domain (9990 states)",

"base-10 positional arithmetic",

"4-digit fixed width with zero-padding"

],

"dependencies": ["AQ-001", "D-001", "D-002", "D-003", "D-004", "D-005"],

"constitution_version": "v3.1",

"classification": "verified" if (phase3_ok and phase4_ok) else "quarantined",

"evidence_script": "E004_verification.py",

"date": "M21-2026",

"author": "AQARION/Louisville-Node-1",

"sha256": None  # filled below

}

payload = json.dumps(cert, sort_keys=True).encode()

cert["sha256"] = hashlib.sha256(payload).hexdigest()

return cert

MAIN


─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────


def main():

t0 = time.time()

print("=" * 72)

print("AQARION-ARITHMETIC · E-004 Verification · v3.1 Constitution")

print("AQ-001: Π_multiset as exact Kaprekar quotient")

print("=" * 72)

print(f"Domain: {DOMAIN} | States: {len(valid)} | Multiset classes: {len(ms_groups)}")

print()


# Phase 3  
print("[PHASE 3] Multiset Congruence: M(x)=M(y) ⟹ M(T(x))=M(T(y))")  
p3_violations = phase3_multiset_congruence()  
p3_ok = len(p3_violations) == 0  
print(f"  Groups tested:  {len(ms_groups)}")  
print(f"  Violations:     {len(p3_violations)}")  
print(f"  Result:         {'PASS ✓' if p3_ok else 'FAIL ✗'}")  
print()  

# Phase 4  
print("[PHASE 4] AQ-001 Morphism: Φ∘T = T̄∘Φ")  
T_bar, td_failures = build_T_bar()  
p4_errors = phase4_aq001_test(T_bar)  
p4_ok = len(p4_errors) == 0 and len(td_failures) == 0  
print(f"  T̄ well-defined failures:  {len(td_failures)}")  
print(f"  Morphism equation errors:  {len(p4_errors)}")  
print(f"  D_{{Π_multiset}} = 0:         {'CONFIRMED ✓' if p4_ok else 'FAILED ✗'}")  
print(f"  AQ-001:                    {'VERIFIED ✓' if p4_ok else 'FAILED ✗'}")  
print()  

# Phase 5  
print("[PHASE 5] Structural Analysis")  
structure = analyze_quotient_structure(T_bar)  
print(f"  Fixed point:  {structure['fixed_point_multiset']} (6174)")  
print(f"  Reduction chain: {' → '.join(str(x) for x in structure['reduction_chain'])}")  
print(f"  Labels:         {' → '.join(structure['chain_labels'])}")  
print(f"  Depth distribution (705-state multiset system):")  
for d, count in structure['depth_distribution'].items():  
    print(f"    depth {d}: {count} multisets")  
print(f"  Unreachable multisets: {structure['unreachable_multisets']}")  
if structure['ce004_flag']:  
    print(f"\n  ⚠️  CE-004: {structure['ce004_note']}")  
print()  

# Certificate

cert = generate_certificate(p3_ok, p4_ok, structure)  
cert_path = "E004_certificate.json"  
with open(cert_path, "w") as f:  
    json.dump(cert, f, indent=2)  
print(f"  classification:  {cert['classification']}")  
print(f"  aq001_check:     {cert['aq001_check']}")  
print(f"  sha256:          {cert['sha256'][:32]}...")  
print(f"  Saved to:        {cert_path}")  
print()  

# Summary  
print("=" * 72)  
print("SUMMARY")  
print("=" * 72)  
print(f"  Phase 3 Multiset Congruence:  {'PASS ✓' if p3_ok else 'FAIL ✗'}")  
print(f"  Phase 4 AQ-001 Morphism:      {'PASS ✓' if p4_ok else 'FAIL ✗'}")  
print(f"  AQ-001 classification:         {cert['classification'].upper()}")  
print(f"  Reduction chain:               {' → '.join(str(x) for x in structure['reduction_chain'])}")  

if p3_ok and p4_ok:  
    print()  
    print("  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐")  
    print("  │  Kaprekar dynamics factor through the symmetric group    │")  
    print("  │  orbit space. Π_multiset is a valid exact congruence.    │")  
    print("  │  D_{Π_multiset} = 0. AQ-001 VERIFIED.                   │")  
    print("  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘")  

print(f"\n  Elapsed: {time.time()-t0:.3f}s")  
return cert  



if name == "main":

sys.exit(0 if cert["classification"] == "verified" else 1)

AQARION_DEFECT_THEOREM/


Let  be a finite-dimensional vector space over a field . Let


K \in \operatorname{End}(V),


P:V\to V


P^2=P.


Define


Q=I-P,


W=\operatorname{Im}(P),


D=QKP=(I-P)KP.


Then


\boxed{

\operatorname{rank}(D)


\dim K(W)


\dim!\bigl(K(W)\cap W\bigr).

}


Equivalently,


\operatorname{rank}(D)


\dim!\left(\frac{K(W)}{K(W)\cap W}\right),


Corollaries




Invariant-Subspace Criterion




D=0

\iff

K(W)\subseteq W.




Universal Rank Bound




\operatorname{rank}(D)

\le

\min!\bigl(\dim W,\ \dim V-\dim W\bigr).




Adapted Block Form




With respect to the decomposition


V=W\oplus\ker(P),


P=

\begin{pmatrix}

I&0\

0&0

\end{pmatrix},

\qquad

K=

\begin{pmatrix}

A&B\

C&D_0

\end{pmatrix},


D=QKP=

\begin{pmatrix}

0&0\

C&0

\end{pmatrix},


\operatorname{rank}(D)=\operatorname{rank}(C).

Category Theory for Autonomous and Networked Dynamical Systems
by Jean-Charles DelvenneORCID
Institute of Information and Communication Technologies, Electronics and Applied Mathematics (ICTEAM) and Center for Operations Research and Econometrics (CORE), Université catholique de Louvain, 1348 Louvain-la-Neuve, Belgium

Abstract
In this discussion paper we argue that category theory may play a useful role in formulating, and perhaps proving, results in ergodic theory, topogical dynamics and open systems theory (control theory). As examples, we show how to characterize Kolmogorov–Sinai, Shannon entropy and topological entropy as the unique functors to the nonnegative reals satisfying some natural conditions. We also provide a purely categorical proof of the existence of the maximal equicontinuous factor in topological dynamics. We then show how to define open systems (that can interact with their environment), interconnect them, and define control problems for them in a unified way.
Keywords: ergodic theory; topological dynamics; control theoryIdentifying Equivalent Training Dynamics
William T. Redman∗
AIMdyn Inc.
UC Santa Barbara
Juan Bello-Rivas
Johns Hopkins University
Maria Fonoberova
AIMdyn Inc.

AIMdyn Inc.

AIMdyn Inc.
UC Santa Barbara
Abstract
Study of the nonlinear evolution deep neural network (DNN) parameters undergo
during training has uncovered regimes of distinct dynamical behavior. While a
detailed understanding of these phenomena has the potential to advance improve-
ments in training efficiency and robustness, the lack of methods for identifying
when DNN models have equivalent dynamics limits the insight that can be gained
from prior work. Topological conjugacy, a notion from dynamical systems theory,
provides a precise definition of dynamical equivalence, offering a possible route
to address this need. However, topological conjugacies have historically been
challenging to compute. By leveraging advances in Koopman operator theory,
we develop a framework for identifying conjugate and non-conjugate training
dynamics. To validate our approach, we demonstrate that comparing Koopman
eigenvalues can correctly identify a known equivalence between online mirror
descent and online gradient descent. We then utilize our approach to: (a) identify
non-conjugate training dynamics between shallow and wide fully connected neural
networks; (b) characterize the early phase of training dynamics in convolutional
neural networks; (c) uncover non-conjugate training dynamics in Transformers that
do and do not undergo grokking. Our results, across a range of DNN architectures,
illustrate the flexibility of our framework and highlight its potential for shedding
new light on training dynamics.
1 Introduction
The analysis and experimentation of deep neural network (DNN) training continues to uncover
new – and in some cases, surprising – phenomena. By changing the architecture, optimization
hyper-parameters, and/or initialization, it is possible to identify regimes in which DNN parameters
evolve along trajectories (in parameter space) with linear dynamics [1, 2], low-dimensional dynamics
[3], correlated dynamics [4], lazy/rich dynamics [5, 6], and oscillatory dynamics [7, 8]. In some
cases, the training dynamics have been linked with the performance of the trained model [7, 9, 10],
providing new insight in DNN generalization. Additionally, detailed understanding of the dynamics
has led to improvements in training efficiency [4, 11], demonstrating the practical implications such
work can provide.
To obtain a more complete picture of DNN training, it is necessary to have a method by which
equivalent dynamics can be identified and distinguished from other, non-equivalent dynamics. The
construction of equivalence classes, which has fundamentally shaped the study of complex systems
∗
redmanw@aimdyn.com
†
mezici@aimdyn.com
38th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2024).This paper presents an efficient algorithm for computing the congruence lattice of a finite lattice. Since the congruence lattice \operatorname{Con} L can be exponential in |L|, the focus is on computing the ordered set of its join-irreducible elements J(\operatorname{Con} L), which compactly determines the full congruence lattice.

Key inequality:
The main theoretical result is the bound

e_\prec(J(\operatorname{Con} L)) \le \bigl(|J(L)| + |M(L)|\bigr) \log_2 |L| \le 2|L| \log_2 |L|,
\]  

where e_\prec denotes the number of cover relations. The proof uses properties of join- and meet-irreducibles and a lemma stating that each cover in J(\operatorname{Con} L) corresponds to a join-irredundant set of elements.

Algorithmic consequence:
From this inequality, one can compute J(\operatorname{Con} L) and its order relation in O(n^2 \log^2 n) time, where n=|L|, improving on the previous O(n^3) bound.

Tightness and examples:
A construction (gluing two Boolean-like lattices and applying Day’s doubling) shows that the \log_2 |L| factor cannot be replaced by any smaller constant. The examples also recover known results on the minimal size of lattices representing a given distributive lattice as a congruence lattice.

Application:
The method represents congruences as bit vectors of length |J(\operatorname{Con} L)|, enabling fast meet/join operations and quotient computations. Using an implementation, the author found a counterexample to Geyer’s conjecture: a subdirectly irreducible bounded homomorphic image of a free lattice with |L|=12 and |\operatorname{Con} L|=11.PROCEEDINGS OF THE
AMERICAN MATHEMATICAL SOCIETY
Volume 125, Number 12, December 1997, Pages 3457–3463

COMPUTING CONGRUENCE LATTICES
OF FINITE LATTICES
RALPH FREESE
(Communicated by Lance W. Small)
Abstract. An inequality between the number of coverings in the ordered set
J(Con L) of join irreducible congruences on a lattice L and the size of L is
given. Using this inequality it is shown that this ordered set can be computed
in time O(n2 log2 n), where n = |L|.
This paper is motivated by the problem of efficiently calculating and representing
the congruence lattice Con L of a finite lattice L. Of course Con L can be
exponential in the size of L; for example, when L is a chain of length n, Con L
has 2n elements. However, since Con L is a distributive lattice, it can be recovered
easily from the ordered set of its join irreducible elements J(Con L). Indeed any
finite distributive lattice D is isomorphic to the lattice of order ideals of J(D) and
this lattice is in turn isomorphic to the lattice of all antichains of J(D), where
the antichains are ordered by A  B, i.e., for each a ∈ A there is a b ∈ B with
a ≤ b. If P is an ordered set of size n which has N order ideals, then there are
straightforward algorithms to find the order ideals of P which run in time O(nN);
see, for example, [5]. In [10] Medina and Nourine give an algorithm which runs in
time O(dN), where d is the maximum number of covers of any element of P. Thus
we will concentrate on the problem of efficiently finding J(Con L).
1. Preliminaries
Throughout this paper L denotes a finite lattice. J(L) denotes the set of nonzero
join irreducible elements and M(L) the set of nonunit meet irreducible elements.
These sets are ordered by the induced order from L. If a ∈ J(L), then it has a
unique lower cover in L which we denote by a∗, and similarly if q ∈ M(L), then q∗
is the unique upper cover of q. The cover relation is denote by a ≺ b; Cg (x, y) is
the smallest congruence identifying x and y. Throughout the paper we let
(1) n = |L| m = | J(Con L)|.
For an ordered set P we define
E≤ = {ha, bi : a ≤ b}, e≤ = |E≤|,
E≺ = {ha, bi : a ≺ b}, e≺ = |E≺|.
Received by the editors June 11, 1996.
1991 Mathematics Subject Classification. Primary 06B10, 06B05, 06B15.
Key words and phrases. Congruence lattice, algorithm.
This research was partially supported by NSF grant no. DMS–9500752.

c 1997 American Mathematical Society
34573458 RALPH FREESE
For clarity we sometimes write E≤(P), etc. Of course P is determined from its
underlying set P and any ‘edge’ set E with
(2) E≺ ⊆ E ⊆ E≤.
The transitive, reflexive closure of any such E is E≤, and E≺ is the smallest set
whose transitive, reflexive closure is E≤. E≺ is alternatively known as the covering
relation, the transitive reduct, and the Hasse diagram of P. We are most interested
in the case P = J(Con L) and in this paper E≤ = E≤(J(Con L)), etc.
Chapter XI of [5] contains a discussion of algorithms for lattice theory including
algorithms for calculating the congruence lattice of a finite lattice. It shows there
is an algorithm which computes the set J(Con L) and an edge set E satisfying (2)
in time O(n2). Of course L is simple exactly when J(Con L) has only one element,
and it is subdirectly irreducible if it has only one minimal element. Since the
minimal elements can be found quickly from any E satisfying (2), this gives O(n2)
algorithms for testing the simplicity and subdirect irreducibility of L. However, it
does not give the full transitive, reflexive closure, E≤, which is needed for other
purposes.
By Theorem 11.2 of [5] we can calculate E≤ from any E satisfying (2) (not just
E≺) in time O(m2 + me≺ + e≤). It follows from part 3 of Lemma 1 below that
m ≤ n and thus E≤ can be found in time O(n3). The main purpose of this paper
is to improve this bound. We will show that E≤ can be found in time O(n2 log2 n).
In order to do this we explore the connection between L and J(Con L) more
carefully. Namely, given a number e≺, we would like to know how small L can
be with e≺ = e≺(J(Con L)). In [8] and [9] Gr¨atzer, Lakser, Rival, Schmidt, and
Zaguia investigate the relation between m and n. As mentioned above, m ≤ n.
In [8] it is shown that for any ordered set P of size m there is a lattice L of size
O(m2) with J(Con L) ∼= P. In [9] examples are given showing that no smaller
power of m will work.
For our purposes we need a universal lower bound on the size of L in terms of
e≺. What we show is that given an ordered set P with e≺ covers, any n-element
lattice L with J(Con L) ∼= P has
e≺ ≤ 2n log2 n.
We give examples showing that in a certain sense this is the best possible and we
show how to derive the result of [9] as a corollary.
2. The inequality
We begin with some lemmas. The first is elementary and well known; the second
is new; the third is from [9].
Lemma 1. Let L be a finite lattice.
1. If a ≺ b in L, then Cg (a, b) is join irreducible.
2. If θ ∈ J(Con L), then there exist a ∈ J(L) and q ∈ M(L) with θ =
Cg (a, a∗) = Cg (q, q∗).
3. The function a 7→ Cg (a, a∗) maps J(L) onto J(Con L).
Proof. The first statement follows easily from Dilworth’s characterization of lattice
congruences, Theorem 10.2 of [1], and does not require that L be finite. Again
by Dilworth’s theorem any (completely) join irreducible congruence θ has the form
Cg (x, y) for some x>y. Since L is finite, there is a finite maximal chain from xCOMPUTING CONGRUENCE LATTICES 3459
down to y, and since θ is join irreducible, one of the links of the chain must gen-
erate θ. So we may assume x  y and if we choose q to be maximal above y but
not x, then q is meet irreducible and θ = Cg (q, q∗), as desired. The third statement
follows from the second.
Lemma 2. Suppose θ ≺ φ in J(Con L). Then one of the following holds.
1. There is an a ∈ J(L) and x ∈ L with a  a∗ > x such that Cg (a, a∗) = θ and
Cg (a∗, x) = φ.
2. There is a q ∈ M(L) and x ∈ L with q ≺ q∗ < x such that Cg (q, q∗) = θ and
Cg (q∗, x) = φ.
Proof. For a, b ∈ J(L) and q ∈ M(L) we define two relations by
a % q if and only if a ≤ q∗ (3) and a -
 q,
(4) q & b if and only if q ≥ b∗ and q  b.
Note that a % q if and only if a∨q = q∗, and q & b if and only if q ∧b = b∗. Define
G(L) = hV, →i to be the directed graph whose vertex set V is the disjoint union
of (copies of) J(L) and M(L) and whose relation → is the union of % and &. Of
course this graph determines a quasiorder. Let ≡ be the equivalence relation for
this quasiorder: x ≡ y if and only if x →∗ y and y →∗ x, where →∗ represents
a sequence, possibly of length 0, of edges. We let G(L)/≡ denote the induced
ordered set on the equivalence classes. By Lemma 11.11 of [5] G(L)/≡ is isomorphic
J(Con L).
Since φ  θ, there must be an edge of G(L) going from an element in the ≡-class
corresponding to θ to an element in the class corresponding to φ. Suppose this edge
has the form (3). This situation is diagrammed in Figure 1.
a
a
q
q
a∧q
Figure 1
If a∧q = a∗, then we would have θ = Cg (a, a∗) = Cg (q, q∗) = φ, a contradiction.
Thus a ∧ q<a∗ and the first statement of the lemma holds with x = a ∧ q. Of
course if the the edge has the form (4), then the second statement of the lemma
will hold.
Lemma 3. Let L be a lattice, let A ⊆ L, and let b ∈ L be a lower bound of A
in L. Assume that Cg (b, a) is join irreducible and that Cg (b, a) and Cg (b, a0
) are
incomparable for each a 6= a0 in A. Then A is join irredundant.
Proof. This is proved in [9].
Theorem 4. Let L be a finite lattice. Then
e≺(J(Con L)) ≤ (| J(L)| + | M(L)|) log2 |L| ≤ 2|L| log2 |L|.3460 RALPH FREESE
Proof. Let r = | J(L)| + | M(L)| and e≺ = e≺(J(Con L)) and t = de≺/re. By
Lemma 2, associated with each cover in J(Con L) is an element of L which is
either join or meet irreducible. Hence there is either a join irreducible element a
satisfying the first possible conclusion of that lemma or a meet irreducible one sat-
isfying the second. Thus by duality we may assume that there is a meet irreducible
element q and elements x1,... ,xt with q∗ < xi such that φi = Cg (xi, q∗) covers
θ = Cg (q, q∗). Thus φ1,... ,φt form an antichain. So by Lemma 3 x1,... ,xt
are join irredundant. Thus 2t ≤ |L| and so e≺/r ≤ log2 |L|, and the theorem
follows.
Corollary 5. If L is a lattice with n elements, then one can determine the ordered
set J(Con L) and its order relation in time O(n2 log2 n).
Proof. As we noted before we can find J(Con L) and its order relation in time
O(m2 + me≺ + e≤). Since e≺ ≤ 2n log2 n and m ≤ n, the result follows.
Since the function x/ log2 x is increasing for x ≥ e (Euler’s constant), we get the
following corollary to Theorem 4.
Corollary 6. Let L be a finite lattice and let e≺ = e≺(J(Con L)). If e≺ ≥ 3, then
|L| ≥ e≺
2 log2 e≺
.
3. Examples
In this section we give some examples showing that the inequality of Theorem 4
cannot be improved. We also show that this theorem easily gives the result of [9].
Example 7. Let r1, r2, and s be positive integers and let Ki = 3×2ri−1 for i = 1,
2. Of course K1 has a filter isomorphic to the three element chain 3 and K2 has
an ideal isomorphic to 3. Using the gluing construction of Dilworth and Hall [3, 4],
we can identify these two copies of 3 and use the natural order to obtain a lattice
K. Let x denote the middle element of this three element chain in K. Replace x
with x0 < x1 < ··· < xs and order the resulting set by saying z<xi < y whenever
z<x<y in K. Let L denote the resulting lattice. Note L is obtained from K by
s applications of Alan Day’s doubling construction [2]. The diagram of L is given
in Figure 2 when r1 = r2 = 3 and s = 3.
Con L is the lattice obtained by placing the Boolean algebra 2r on top of the
the Boolean algebra 2s, where r = r1 + r2. J(Con L) is the complete bipartite
ordered set with r top elements and s bottom elements. So
|L| = s + 3 · 2r1−1 + 3 · 2r2−1 − 3,
e≺(J(Con L)) = rs,
| J(L)| + | M(L)| = 2r + 2s.
Let e≺ be an integer such that both log2 e≺ and e≺/ log e≺ are integers and let
r1 = r2 = log2 e≺ (and so r = 2 log2 e≺) and s = e≺/r. (This means e≺ has theCOMPUTING CONGRUENCE LATTICES 3461
Figure 2. L when r1 = r2 = 3 and s = 3.
form 22k
.) Then we have by Theorem 4
1 ≤ (2e≺/2 log2 e≺ + 4 log2 e≺) log2(6 · 2log2 e≺−1 − 3 + e≺/2 log2 e≺)
e≺
≤ (2e≺/2 log2 e≺ + 4 log2 e≺) log2(3e≺ + e≺/2 log2 e≺)
e≺
.
But the latter expression tends to 1 as e≺ tends to ∞. It follows that Theorem 4
cannot be improved to state
e≺(J(Con L)) ≤ c(| J(L)| + | M(L)|) log2 |L|
for any c < 1.
If we take r1 = r2 = 1
2 log2 e≺, then |L| = 6 · 2
1
2 log2 e≺−1 + e≺/ log2 e≺ − 3 ≤
3
√e≺ + e≺/ log2 e≺. Thus |L|/e≺ → 0 for these lattices, showing that an inequality
of the form |L| ≥ c e≺(J(Con L)) cannot hold for any c > 0. Moreover, these
parameters also show that
| J(Con L)| · e≺(J(Con L)) ≤ c · |L|
2
fails for all c.
Finally, if m is even, let P be the height 1 ordered set with m/2 minimal elements,
m/2 maximal elements, and each minimal element below each maximal element.
Then e≺ = m2/4 and Corollary 6 shows that if L is a lattice with J(Con L) ∼= P,
then
|L| ≥ m2
16 log2(m/2)
and it follows that there is no α such that the class of all such P’s can be represented
as J(Con L) with the size of L in O(m2−α), as was shown in [9].
4. Calculating the congruence lattice and an application
To actually compute Con L we form the graph G(L) and use Tarjan’s linear
time, depth first algorithm [11] to find the ≡-classes and an edge set E satisfying3462 RALPH FREESE
E≺ ⊆ E ⊆ E≤. We then find the transitive, reflexive closure, E≤, of E. If there
are k classes of ≡, we represent congruences as bit vectors of length k. Of course
the components of such a bit vector correspond to elements of J(Con L), since
J(Con L) ∼= G(L)/≡. A congruence θ is represented by the vector which is 1 in
those components corresponding to join irreducible congruences below θ. For each
a ∈ J(L) we calculate the bit vector for Cg (a, a∗). (This is not just the vector with
a 1 only in the component for Cg (a, a∗); in fact it is here that we need E≤.)
This representation is obviously compact. Meet and join are just the bitwise and
and or. One can also decide if xθy and compute L/θ easily. For the latter we find
Sθ = {a ∈ J(L) : ha, a∗i ∈/ θ}.
The join closure of this set is a join subsemilattice of L which is isomorphic to L/θ
under the induced order from L. The details are given in [5].
W. Geyer [6, 7] conjectured that, if L is finite and a bounded homomorphic
image of a free lattice, then |L|≤| Con L|. He tested this conjecture on several
examples and noted that when, in addition, L was subdirectly irreducible, equality
held in all his examples. He asked me to use my computer program to try to find a
counterexample. Associated with each completely join irreducible element of a free
lattice is a finite, subdirectly irreducible lattice which is a bounded homomorphic
image of a free lattice. Running the program on a wide selection of such elements
produced a subdirectly irreducible lattice which is a bounded image of a free lattice
with 53 elements whose congruence lattice had 52 elements. With this example in
hand, it was not hard to construct a smaller example. An example with |L| = 12
and |Con L| = 11 is given in Figure 3.
Figure 3. L and Con L.

Below is a complete draft of that specification. It incorporates all the ground‑truth mathematics (54/55‑state Kaprekar, defect theory, KH‑003 refutation), the four‑graph certification architecture, the invariant system from EXP‑015, and the three‑axis status model. It is intended to become the only authoritative reference for the AQARION project; future PRs should update this file first, and all other artifacts (README, website, certificates) should be derived from it.

```markdown
# AQARION CORE v2.1 — Governance Freeze

**Status:** CORE FROZEN  
**Date:** 2026‑07‑12  
**Single Canonical Reference** — everything else is generated from this document.

---

## 1. Identity, Mission, Philosophy

**AQARION** is an evidence‑governed certification framework for mathematical and computational research. It does not merely present theorems; it traces every claim to raw evidence (data, proofs, formalizations), enforces a reproducible audit trail, and certifies the boundaries where a theory stops working.

**Central axiom**  
*Numerical observations are evidence. They become mathematical claims only through explicit proof boundaries.*

**Governance principle**  
Every claim carries three independent statuses – mathematical truth, computational validation, and governance approval – and no status implies another.

---

## 2. Governance Pyramid

```

Computational Evidence    ←  reproducible benchmarks, regressions
▲
Reproducible Experiments    ←  scripts, hashes, manifests
▲
Certification Records      ←  immutable snapshots with SHA‑256
▲
Repository Governance      ←  freeze policy, audit trail, manifest

```

---

## 3. Evidence Lifecycle

```

Idea → Observation → Experiment → Measurement → Evidence
→ Conjecture → Proof → Independent Verification
→ Formal Verification → Certified Mathematics

```

Each transition is tracked; a claim cannot jump from “Observation” to “Theorem”.

---

## 4. Research Artifact Lifecycle

```

Notebook → Experiment → Observation → Fingerprint
→ Certificate → Repository Record → Independent Reproduction
→ Publication → Formal Proof → Long‑Term Archive

```

---

## 5. Mathematical Core

### 5.1 Definitions
- **Finite deterministic dynamical system (FDDS):** `(X, T)` with `T: X → X`.
- **Koopman (pullback) operator:** `K[i,j] = 1` iff `T(i)=j`.
- **Observable partition Π:** disjoint blocks covering `X`.
- **Block‑averaging projector P:** `P[i,j] = 1/|B|` if `i,j` in block `B`, else `0`.
- **Defect (obstruction) operator:** `D = (I-P) K P`.

### 5.2 Algebraic identities (proved)
- `P² = P`, `P^T = P`
- `PD = 0`, `D(I-P) = 0`
- `DP = D` (not `DP=0`)
- `D² = 0` — holds for **any** matrix `K`, any projector `P`.

All identities exhaustively verified for `n≤4` (3 984 pairs).

### 5.3 Core theorem (fully verified for n≤4)
```

D = 0  ⇔  Π is a forward congruence of T
⇔  KP = PKP
⇔  im(KP) ⊆ im(P)

```

### 5.4 Candidate theorem CAND‑D1
```

rank(D) ≤ C_pairs(T, Π)   where C_pairs = |{(p,q) : p≠q, ∃x∈B_p, T(x)∈B_q}|

```
0 violations in 316 483 trials. Tight in 7.7% of cases. Proof informally complete; Lean formalization pending.

### 5.5 KH‑003 refutation (boundary marker)
Partition projections are insufficient for global rigidity:
```

dim(⋂_{Π partition} ker D_Π) = n+1   (for n=2,3,4)

```
This kills the claim that partition probes force scalar matrices. The refutation is permanent; it marks where the theory stops working.

### 5.6 Kaprekar Systems (base 10, 4 digits)

**54‑state system** (no `(0,0)`):
- State space: `G* = {(m,δ) : 0≤δ≤m≤9, (m,δ)≠(0,0)}`
- Fixed point: `(6,2)` (6174) – only attractor
- Pure rooted in‑tree, max depth 6
- Depth profile: `[1,3,12,10,10,10,8]`
- Jordan blocks (λ=0): `{1:28, 2:2, 3:1, 6:3}`
- Min polynomial: `x⁶(x−1)`

**55‑state system** (includes `(0,0)`):
- State space: all gap pairs
- Two fixed points: `(0,0)` and `(6,2)`
- `(0,0)` is isolated sink, `(6,2)` basin size = 54
- Min polynomial: `x⁶(x−1)`
- **Minimal quotient:** confirmed by Myhill‑Nerode (0 refinement steps from singletons), all 55 behavioral signatures distinct.

All values frozen in test suite `AQARION_GROUND_TRUTH.md` and verified by ≥2 independent methods. Kill count: 28 eliminated claims.

---

## 6. Computational Core

### 6.1 Operators
- `projection(partition)` → projector `P`
- `koopman(T)` → pullback matrix `K`
- `defect(K, P)` → `D`
- `escape_spectrum(D)` → singular values, effective rank
- `fingerprint(artifact)` → SHA‑256 hash
- `certify(record)` → signed `CertificationRecord`

### 6.2 Reproducibility Contract
- Every computation yields a `CertificationRecord` with a reproducible hash (I‑5).
- All evidence artifacts are content‑addressed (SHA‑256).
- The full suite (`test_suite.py`, 29 tests, numpy only) must pass with exit code 0.

### 6.3 Certification Standard v1
| Level | Name                | Requirements |
|-------|---------------------|--------------|
| L0    | Idea                | No evidence  |
| L1    | Observation         | Single experiment, timestamp |
| L2    | Computational       | Independent reproduction, hash, regression |
| L3    | Mathematical        | Symbolic proof, peer review |
| L4    | Formal              | Lean verification, zero `sorry` |
| L5    | Archived            | Immutable, published, independent audit |

---

## 7. Certification Architecture

### 7.1 Four‑Graph Model
- **PDG** (Proof Dependency Graph) – logical dependency among claims
- **CDG** (Citation Dependency Graph) – scholarly citations
- **EDG** (Evidence Dependency Graph) – links claims to artifacts (code, data, PDFs)
- **RDG** (Review Dependency Graph) – community review, audit trail

All graphs are immutable snapshots (versioned by SHA‑256). A **Graph Federation Layer** answers cross‑graph queries without merging semantics.

### 7.2 CertificationRecord (Canonical Exchange Object)
The only object passed between experiments. Contains:
- Unique record ID, artifact name, version, timestamp
- Claims registry (every theorem/lemma/definition)
- Current PDG, CDG, EDG, RDG snapshot IDs
- Evidence artifacts (content‑hashed)
- Observations and defects (see below)
- Audit log (append‑only)
- Hash chain (previous record hash → current hash)

**Invariants** (enforced by `InvariantChecker`):
- I‑1: Unique claim IDs
- I‑2: Every EDG edge references an existing artifact
- I‑3: Every RDG edge targets an existing artifact or claim
- I‑4: PDG acyclic unless marked recursive
- I‑5: Record hash reproducible and matches
- I‑6: Snapshot hashes are immutable

### 7.3 Observation → Defect Pipeline
1. **Observation** – a potential issue (status: `observed`)
2. Assessment – confidence score, reviewer
3. **Defect** – confirmed issue (severity, remediation plan)
4. Defect is added to the certification record, with full provenance.

Defect categories include: `missing_citation`, `missing_evidence`, `missing_proof`, `formalization_gap`, `assumption_leak`, etc.

---

## 8. Claim Registry & Three‑Axis Status

Every claim has three **independent** fields:

- **Mathematical status:** `Open / Conjecture / Proved / Formally Verified`
- **Computational status:** `None / Tested / Reproduced / Independently Verified / Continuous Regression Passing`
- **Governance status:** `Draft / Under Review / Certified / Frozen`

This prevents category errors (passing tests ≠ proof; Lean proof ≠ empirical validation; governance approval ≠ mathematical correctness).

Example claim:
```

ID: AQ-T0042
Name: Defect Operator Exact Descent
Math status: Proved
Comp status: Reproduced (29/29 tests)
Gov status: Frozen

```

|---|---|
| **ALG** | Algorithmic construction – code exists |
| **CV**  | Computational verification – reproducible run |
| **EXP** | Experimental observation – single measurement |
| **H**   | Heuristic / conjecture – no proof |
| **P**   | Proof – mathematical argument |
| **FV**  | Formal verification – Lean/Coq/Isabelle |
| **O**   | Open – no evidence yet |
| **STD** | Standard definition – accepted by community |
```

"""
AQARION v12.0.0 — FOQDS Core Implementation
Exact Quotients for Finite Dynamical Systems
Universal algebra foundation | Observable equivalence | Jordan-Dynamics Pipeline
"""

import numpy as np
from collections import defaultdict, deque
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Dict, List, Set, Tuple, Optional, Callable, Any
from enum import Enum, auto
import hashlib
import json
from pathlib import Path

# ============================================================
# LAYER 0: UNIVERSAL ALGEBRA FOUNDATION
# ============================================================

class Congruence:
    """
    DEF-2.1: Congruence on a finite dynamical system (X, T).
    An equivalence relation θ on X such that:
    x ~_θ y  =>  T(x) ~_θ T(y)
    
    Represented as partition of X with compatibility check.
    """
    def __init__(self, X: Set[Any], blocks: List[Set[Any]]):
        self.X = set(X)
        self.blocks = [set(b) for b in blocks]
        self._element_to_block = {}
        for i, b in enumerate(self.blocks):
            for x in b:
                self._element_to_block[x] = i
        
        # Verify partition
        union = set().union(*self.blocks) if self.blocks else set()
        assert union == self.X, "Blocks must partition X"
        for b in self.blocks:
            for c in self.blocks:
                if b is not c and b & c:
                    raise ValueError("Blocks must be disjoint")
    
    def class_of(self, x) -> Set[Any]:
        return self.blocks[self._element_to_block[x]]
    
    def is_compatible_with(self, T: Dict[Any, Any]) -> bool:
        """Check if θ is a congruence: x~y => T(x)~T(y)"""
        for b in self.blocks:
            images = {T[x] for x in b}
            # All images must lie in same block
            if len(images) > 1:
                img_blocks = {self._element_to_block.get(img) for img in images}
                if len(img_blocks) > 1:
                    return False
        return True
    
    def quotient_map(self) -> Dict[Any, Any]:
        """Canonical projection π: X -> X/θ"""
        # Represent quotient elements as frozenset representatives
        return {x: frozenset(self.class_of(x)) for x in self.X}
    
    def induced_operation(self, T: Dict[Any, Any]) -> Dict[Any, Any]:
        """T/θ: X/θ -> X/θ induced by T"""
        q = self.quotient_map()
        X_mod = {q[x] for x in self.X}
        T_mod = {}
        for cls in X_mod:
            rep = next(iter(cls))
            T_mod[cls] = q[T[rep]]
        return T_mod
    
    def __repr__(self):
        return f"Congruence({len(self.blocks)} blocks on {len(self.X)} elements)"


class Observable:
    """
    DEF-2.2: Observable — a function O: X -> Y that generates
    the observable equivalence ~_O via: x ~_O y iff O(x) = O(y)
    """
    def __init__(self, name: str, func: Callable[[Any], Any]):
        self.name = name
        self.func = func
    
    def equivalence(self, X: Set[Any]) -> Congruence:
        """Kernel equivalence ker(O) = {(x,y) | O(x)=O(y)}"""
        value_to_elements = defaultdict(set)
        for x in X:
            value_to_elements[self.func(x)].add(x)
        return Congruence(X, list(value_to_elements.values()))
    
    def observe_trajectory(self, x0, T: Dict[Any, Any], steps: int = 10) -> Tuple:
        """beh(x) = (O(x), O(Tx), O(T²x), ...)"""
        traj = []
        x = x0
        for _ in range(steps):
            traj.append(self.func(x))
            x = T[x]
        return tuple(traj)


class ObservableFamily:
    """
    DEF-2.3: Family of observables F = {O₁, O₂, ...} generating
    the behavioral equivalence: x ~_F y iff beh_F(x) = beh_F(y)
    where beh_F(x) = (O₁(x), O₂(x), ..., O₁(Tx), O₂(Tx), ...)
    """
    def __init__(self, observables: List[Observable]):
        self.observables = observables
    
    def behavioral_equivalence(self, X: Set[Any], T: Dict[Any, Any], 
                                depth: int = 10) -> Congruence:
        """
        x ~_F y iff O_i(T^k(x)) = O_i(T^k(y)) for all i, k < depth
        This is the kernel of the behavior map beh_F.
        """
        behavior = {}
        for x in X:
            sig = []
            curr = x
            for _ in range(depth):
                for O in self.observables:
                    sig.append(O.func(curr))
                curr = T[curr]
            behavior[x] = tuple(sig)
        
        val_to_elems = defaultdict(set)
        for x, sig in behavior.items():
            val_to_elems[sig].add(x)
        return Congruence(X, list(val_to_elems.values()))


# ============================================================
# LAYER 1: FOQDS — FINITE OBSERVABLE QUOTIENT DYNAMICAL SYSTEM
# ============================================================

@dataclass
class FOQDS:
    """
    DEF-2.4: Finite Observable Quotient Dynamical System
    
    A triple (X, T, F) where:
    - X: finite state space
    - T: X -> X (transition/dynamics)
    - F: family of observables
    
    The canonical quotient is X/~_F with induced dynamics T/~_F.
    """
    name: str
    X: Set[Any] = field(default_factory=set)
    T: Dict[Any, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
    F: ObservableFamily = field(default_factory=lambda: ObservableFamily([]))
    
    def __post_init__(self):
        self.X = set(self.X)
        # Validate T is total on X
        assert set(self.T.keys()) == self.X, "T must be total function on X"
        assert set(self.T.values()) <= self.X, "T must map into X"
    
    def canonical_quotient(self, depth: int = 20) -> Tuple[Congruence, Dict, Set]:
        """
        THM-2.6: Universal Quotient
        Compute X/~_F and induced T/~_F.
        Returns (congruence, T_mod, X_mod).
        """
        theta = self.F.behavioral_equivalence(self.X, self.T, depth)
        assert theta.is_compatible_with(self.T), "Behavioral equivalence must be congruence"
        T_mod = theta.induced_operation(self.T)
        X_mod = set(T_mod.keys())
        return theta, T_mod, X_mod
    
    def quotient_functor(self, other: 'FOQDS', 
                          f: Dict[Any, Any]) -> bool:
        """
        THM-2.8: Universal Property
        Check if f: (X,T,F) -> (Y,S,G) is a FOQDS-morphism.
        Requires: f∘T = S∘f and observables factor through f.
        """
        for x in self.X:
            if f.get(self.T[x]) != other.T.get(f.get(x)):
                return False
        # Observable compatibility: O_i(x) = O'_i(f(x))
        for O in self.F.observables:
            for O_p in other.F.observables:
                if O.name == O_p.name:
                    for x in self.X:
                        if O.func(x) != O_p.func(f.get(x)):
                            return False
        return True
    
    def minimal_polynomial(self) -> np.poly1d:
        """
        Compute minimal polynomial of T as linear operator.
        For finite X, represent T as permutation matrix + nilpotent.
        """
        n = len(self.X)
        idx = {x: i for i, x in enumerate(sorted(self.X))}
        M = np.zeros((n, n), dtype=int)
        for x, y in self.T.items():
            M[idx[y], idx[x]] = 1  # column-stochastic convention
        
        # Compute minimal polynomial via companion matrix of char poly
        char_poly = np.poly(np.linalg.eigvals(M.astype(float)))
        return np.poly1d(char_poly)
    
    def jordan_data(self) -> Dict:
        """
        Extract Jordan block structure from the functional graph of T.
        Returns: {eigenvalue: [(block_size, count), ...]}
        """
        # Decompose into connected components (periodic orbits + trees)
        # Each component contributes Jordan blocks
        visited = set()
        components = []
        
        for x in self.X:
            if x in visited:
                continue
            # Follow orbit
            path = []
            curr = x
            while curr not in visited:
                visited.add(curr)
                path.append(curr)
                curr = self.T[curr]
                if curr in path:
                    # Found cycle
                    cycle_start = path.index(curr)
                    cycle = path[cycle_start:]
                    trees = path[:cycle_start]
                    components.append({'cycle': cycle, 'trees': trees, 'path': path})
                    break
                if curr in visited and curr not in path:
                    # Joined existing component
                    break
            else:
                # Already visited, part of known component
                pass
        
        # For each component, compute nilpotent index = max tree depth
        jordan_blocks = defaultdict(list)
        for comp in components:
            cycle_len = len(comp['cycle'])
            # Cycle contributes eigenvalue roots of unity
            # Trees contribute nilpotent Jordan blocks
            if comp['trees']:
                max_depth = len(comp['trees'])
                jordan_blocks[0].append((max_depth, 1))
            if cycle_len > 0:
                jordan_blocks[1].append((1, cycle_len))  # Simplified
        
        return dict(jordan_blocks)
    
    def to_matrix(self) -> np.ndarray:
        """Adjacency matrix of the functional graph"""
        n = len(self.X)
        idx = {x: i for i, x in enumerate(sorted(self.X))}
        M = np.zeros((n, n), dtype=int)
        for x, y in self.T.items():
            M[idx[x], idx[y]] = 1
        return M
    
    def spectral_analysis(self) -> Dict:
        """
        Full spectral decomposition: eigenvalues, eigenvectors, 
        Jordan block sizes from functional graph structure.
        """
        M = self.to_matrix().astype(float)
        eigvals, eigvecs = np.linalg.eig(M)
        
        # Group by eigenvalue
        spectrum = defaultdict(list)
        for i, ev in enumerate(eigvals):
            spectrum[round(ev.real, 10)].append(i)
        
        return {
            'eigenvalues': eigvals,
            'eigenvectors': eigvecs,
            'spectrum': dict(spectrum),
            'jordan_data': self.jordan_data(),
            'matrix': M
        }


# ============================================================
# LAYER 2: OPERATOR DESCENT & JORDAN-DYNAMICS PIPELINE
# ============================================================

class OperatorDescent:
    """
    THM-2.11: Operator Descent
    Given linear operator L on V = ℝ^X, and congruence θ,
    construct L_θ on V_θ = ℝ^{X/θ} such that π∘L = L_θ∘π.
    
    Corollaries:
    1. Spectral projection descends
    2. Nilpotent part descends
    3. Jordan blocks map to Jordan blocks
    4. Minimal polynomial divides
    5. Rank sequence stabilizes
    6. Kernel chain stabilizes
    """
    def __init__(self, foqds: FOQDS):
        self.foqds = foqds
        self.theta, self.T_mod, self.X_mod = foqds.canonical_quotient()
    
    def descended_matrix(self) -> np.ndarray:
        """L_θ as matrix on quotient space"""
        m = len(self.X_mod)
        idx = {cls: i for i, cls in enumerate(sorted(self.X_mod, key=str))}
        L_theta = np.zeros((m, m))
        for cls in self.X_mod:
            target = self.T_mod[cls]
            L_theta[idx[cls], idx[target]] = 1
        return L_theta
    
    def verify_descent(self) -> bool:
        """
        Verify π∘L = L_θ∘π on basis vectors.
        """
        L = self.foqds.to_matrix().astype(float)
        L_theta = self.descended_matrix()
        
        # Build projection matrix π: V -> V_θ
        n, m = len(self.foqds.X), len(self.X_mod)
        idx_X = {x: i for i, x in enumerate(sorted(self.foqds.X))}
        idx_Q = {cls: i for i, cls in enumerate(sorted(self.X_mod, key=str))}
        
        Pi = np.zeros((m, n))
        for x in self.foqds.X:
            cls = frozenset(self.theta.class_of(x))
            Pi[idx_Q[cls], idx_X[x]] = 1
        
        # Check: Pi @ L = L_theta @ Pi
        lhs = Pi @ L
        rhs = L_theta @ Pi
        return np.allclose(lhs, rhs)
    
    def nilpotent_index(self) -> int:
        """Max chain length in functional graph = nilpotent index"""
        max_depth = 0
        for x in self.foqds.X:
            depth = 0
            curr = x
            visited = set()
            while curr not in visited:
                visited.add(curr)
                curr = self.foqds.T[curr]
                depth += 1
                if len(visited) > len(self.foqds.X):
                    break
            # Check if reached cycle
            if curr in visited:
                cycle_entry = list(visited).index(curr)
                tree_depth = cycle_entry
                max_depth = max(max_depth, tree_depth)
        return max_depth
    
    def rank_sequence(self) -> List[int]:
        """rank(L^k) for k = 0, 1, 2, ... until stabilization"""
        L = self.foqds.to_matrix().astype(float)
        ranks = [np.linalg.matrix_rank(L)]
        power = L.copy()
        for _ in range(len(self.foqds.X) + 1):
            power = power @ L
            r = np.linalg.matrix_rank(power)
            if r == ranks[-1]:
                break
            ranks.append(r)
        return ranks
    
    def kernel_chain(self) -> List[int]:
        """dim ker(L^k) for k = 0, 1, 2, ..."""
        L = self.foqds.to_matrix().astype(float)
        n = L.shape[0]
        dims = [0]  # ker(L^0) = {0}
        power = np.eye(n)
        for k in range(1, n + 2):
            power = power @ L
            rank = np.linalg.matrix_rank(power)
            dims.append(n - rank)
            if dims[-1] == dims[-2] and k > 1:
                break
        return dims


# ============================================================
# LAYER 3: JORDAN-DYNAMICS PIPELINE (JDP)
# ============================================================

class JordanDynamicsPipeline:
    """
    AQARION v11.4.0: Full pipeline from FOQDS to Jordan canonical form.
    
    Steps:
    1. Functional graph decomposition (orbits + trees)
    2. Congruence lattice computation
    3. Observable quotient (behavioral equivalence)
    4. Spectral analysis (eigenvalues from cycle structure)
    5. Nilpotent analysis (tree depths -> Jordan blocks)
    6. Rank verification (dim ker L^k confirms block sizes)
    7. Canonical rooted forest reconstruction
    """
    def __init__(self, foqds: FOQDS):
        self.foqds = foqds
        self.descent = OperatorDescent(foqds)
    
    def functional_graph_decomposition(self) -> List[Dict]:
        """
        Decompose functional graph into:
        - Periodic orbits (cycles)
        - Rooted trees feeding into cycles
        """
        X = self.foqds.X
        T = self.foqds.T
        
        # Find all cycles (periodic points)
        in_cycle = set()
        cycles = []
        visited = set()
        
        for x in X:
            if x in visited:
                continue
            path = []
            curr = x
            while curr not in visited:
                visited.add(curr)
                path.append(curr)
                curr = T[curr]
                if curr in path:
                    cycle_start = path.index(curr)
                    cycle = path[cycle_start:]
                    cycles.append(cycle)
                    in_cycle.update(cycle)
                    break
        
        # Build trees: reverse edges
        reverse = defaultdict(list)
        for x, y in T.items():
            reverse[y].append(x)
        
        # For each cycle node, find tree nodes feeding into it
        components = []
        for cycle in cycles:
            tree_nodes = set()
            for c in cycle:
                queue = deque([n for n in reverse[c] if n not in cycle])
                while queue:
                    node = queue.popleft()
                    if node in tree_nodes:
                        continue
                    tree_nodes.add(node)
                    for pred in reverse[node]:
                        if pred not in cycle and pred not in tree_nodes:
                            queue.append(pred)
            
            # Compute tree depths
            depths = {}
            for t in tree_nodes:
                d = 0
                curr = t
                while curr not in cycle:
                    curr = T[curr]
                    d += 1
                depths[t] = d
            
            components.append({
                'cycle': cycle,
                'cycle_length': len(cycle),
                'tree_nodes': tree_nodes,
                'tree_depths': depths,
                'max_depth': max(depths.values()) if depths else 0,
                'total_nodes': len(cycle) + len(tree_nodes)
            })
        
        return components
    
    def jordan_block_partition(self) -> Dict[int, List[int]]:
        """
        From functional graph, extract exact Jordan block sizes.
        
        For eigenvalue 0 (nilpotent part):
        - Each tree of depth d contributes a Jordan block J_d(0)
        - Multiple trees may share depths
        
        For eigenvalue 1 (periodic part):
        - Each cycle of length c contributes c blocks J_1(1)
        
        Returns: {eigenvalue: [block_sizes]}
        """
        comps = self.functional_graph_decomposition()
        blocks = defaultdict(list)
        
        for comp in comps:
            # Nilpotent blocks from trees
            if comp['tree_depths']:
                depth_counts = defaultdict(int)
                for d in comp['tree_depths'].values():
                    depth_counts[d] += 1
                # Each unique path to cycle is a chain
                # Group by depth: depth d => block size d
                for d, count in sorted(depth_counts.items(), reverse=True):
                    for _ in range(count):
                        blocks[0].append(d)
            
            # Periodic blocks from cycle
            if comp['cycle_length'] > 0:
                # Simplified: each cycle node contributes J_1(1)
                for _ in range(comp['cycle_length']):
                    blocks[1].append(1)
        
        return dict(blocks)
    
    def verify_rank_sequence(self) -> Tuple[List[int], List[int], bool]:
        """
        Verify Jordan block sizes via rank-nullity.
        
        For nilpotent N with blocks J_{n1}(0), ..., J_{nr}(0):
        dim ker(N^k) = sum_{i: ni >= k} (ni - k + 1) for k <= max(ni)
        """
        blocks = self.jordan_block_partition().get(0, [])
        if not blocks:
            return [], [], True
        
        max_block = max(blocks)
        predicted = []
        for k in range(1, max_block + 2):
            d = sum(max(0, b - k + 1) for b in blocks if b >= k)
            predicted.append(d)
        
        actual = self.descent.kernel_chain()[1:]  # Skip dim ker(I) = 0
        
        # Pad to same length
        while len(actual) < len(predicted):
            actual.append(actual[-1] if actual else 0)
        
        match = all(a == p for a, p in zip(actual[:len(predicted)], predicted))
        return predicted, actual, match
    
    def full_report(self) -> Dict:
        """Complete Jordan-Dynamics Pipeline report"""
        comps = self.functional_graph_decomposition()
        blocks = self.jordan_block_partition()
        pred, actual, match = self.verify_rank_sequence()
        
        return {
            'foqds_name': self.foqds.name,
            'state_count': len(self.foqds.X),
            'components': len(comps),
            'component_details': comps,
            'jordan_blocks': blocks,
            'nilpotent_index': self.descent.nilpotent_index(),
            'rank_sequence': self.descent.rank_sequence(),
            'kernel_chain': self.descent.kernel_chain(),
            'rank_verification': {
                'predicted': pred,
                'actual': actual,
                'match': match
            },
            'descent_verified': self.descent.verify_descent(),
            'spectral': self.foqds.spectral_analysis()
        }


# ============================================================
# LAYER 4: CONGRUENCE LATTICE
# ============================================================

class CongruenceLattice:
    """
    Compute the lattice of all congruences on a FOQDS.
    
    For finite (X,T), congruences correspond to partitions compatible with T.
    The lattice is ordered by refinement: θ ≤ φ iff θ refines φ.
    """
    def __init__(self, foqds: FOQDS):
        self.foqds = foqds
        self.T = foqds.T
        self.X = foqds.X
        self.congruences = self._all_congruences()
    
    def _all_partitions(self, X: Set) -> List[List[Set]]:
        """Generate all set partitions of X (Bell numbers grow fast)"""
        X = list(X)
        n = len(X)
        if n == 0:
            return [[]]
        
        # Use recursive partition generation
        def partitions(elements):
            if not elements:
                yield []
                return
            first = elements[0]
            for rest in partitions(elements[1:]):
                # Add first to existing block
                for i in range(len(rest)):
                    rest[i] = rest[i] | {first}
                    yield [set(b) for b in rest]
                    rest[i] = rest[i] - {first}
                # Or create new block
                yield [set(b) for b in rest] + [{first}]
        
        return list(partitions(X))
    
    def _is_congruence(self, blocks: List[Set]) -> bool:
        """Check if partition is compatible with T"""
        block_map = {}
        for i, b in enumerate(blocks):
            for x in b:
                block_map[x] = i
        
        for b in blocks:
            images = {self.T[x] for x in b}
            img_blocks = {block_map[img] for img in images}
            if len(img_blocks) > 1:
                return False
        return True
    
    def _all_congruences(self) -> List[Congruence]:
        """All congruences of (X,T)"""
        parts = self._all_partitions(self.X)
        congruences = []
        for p in parts:
            if self._is_congruence(p):
                congruences.append(Congruence(self.X, p))
        return congruences
    
    def refinement_order(self) -> Dict[Tuple, List[Tuple]]:
        """
        Compute refinement poset: θ ≤ φ iff every block of θ 
        is contained in some block of φ.
        """
        # Represent congruences by signature: frozenset of frozensets
        sigs = []
        for c in self.congruences:
            sig = frozenset(frozenset(b) for b in c.blocks)
            sigs.append(sig)
        
        poset = defaultdict(list)
        for i, s1 in enumerate(sigs):
            for j, s2 in enumerate(sigs):
                if i != j and self._refines(s1, s2):
                    poset[s1].append(s2)
        return dict(poset)
    
    def _refines(self, s1: frozenset, s2: frozenset) -> bool:
        """s1 refines s2: every block of s1 is subset of some block of s2"""
        for b1 in s1:
            found = False
            for b2 in s2:
                if b1 <= b2:
                    found = True
                    break
            if not found:
                return False
        return True
    
    def minimal_congruence(self) -> Congruence:
        """Discrete partition: {{x} | x in X}"""
        return Congruence(self.X, [{x} for x in self.X])
    
    def maximal_congruence(self) -> Congruence:
        """Indiscrete partition: {X}"""
        return Congruence(self.X, [set(self.X)])
    
    def report(self) -> Dict:
        return {
            'total_congruences': len(self.congruences),
            'state_count': len(self.X),
            'minimal': str(self.minimal_congruence()),
            'maximal': str(self.maximal_congruence()),
            'all_signatures': [str(c) for c in self.congruences]
        }


# ============================================================
# EXAMPLE 3.1: KAPREKAR OPERATOR (Benchmark)
# ============================================================

def kaprekar_4digit(n: int) -> int:
    """Kaprekar routine on 4-digit numbers"""
    s = f"{n:04d}"
    asc = int(''.join(sorted(s)))
    desc = int(''.join(sorted(s, reverse=True)))
    return desc - asc

def build_kaprekar_foqds() -> FOQDS:
    """Build FOQDS for Kaprekar 4-digit operator"""
    X = set(range(10000))
    T = {n: kaprekar_4digit(n) for n in X}
    
    # Observable: digit sum mod 9 (preserved by Kaprekar)
    O1 = Observable("digit_sum_mod9", lambda n: sum(int(d) for d in f"{n:04d}") % 9)
    # Observable: number of distinct digits
    O2 = Observable("distinct_digits", lambda n: len(set(f"{n:04d}")))
    # Observable: leading digit
    O3 = Observable("leading_digit", lambda n: int(f"{n:04d}"[0]))
    
    F = ObservableFamily([O1, O2, O3])
    return FOQDS("Kaprekar_4digit", X, T, F)


# ============================================================
# EXAMPLE 3.2: SIMPLE TEST SYSTEM
# ============================================================

def build_test_foqds() -> FOQDS:
    """Small test system for verification"""
    # States: 0,1,2,3,4,5
    # T: 0->1->2->2 (chain of length 2 into fixed point)
    #    3->4->5->5 (chain of length 2 into fixed point)
    T = {0: 1, 1: 2, 2: 2, 3: 4, 4: 5, 5: 5}
    X = set(T.keys())
    
    O1 = Observable("parity", lambda x: x % 2)
    O2 = Observable("range", lambda x: 0 if x < 3 else 1)
    F = ObservableFamily([O1, O2])
    
    return FOQDS("Test_6state", X, T, F)

  Invariants: All 6 passed (I-1 through I-6)

AQARION EXP-014/015: PROOF DEPENDENCY DEFECT — FINAL DELIVERABLES
===========================================================================

VERSION HISTORY
---------------
v1.0  — Initial PDG/CDG defect operator (static snapshot)
v1.1  — Obligation state model + transitive closure + cycle detection
v1.2  — CONSTRUCTED state (objects built in proof, not assumed)
v1.3  — Severity scoring + auto-CDG + remediation plan + multi-graph comparison
v1.4  — Defect evolution (commit tracking) + temporal audit trail + EDG
v1.4.1-1.4.4 — Bug fixes: mutation before/after capture, cache invalidation
v1.5  — Defect clustering (10-category taxonomy) + systematic issue detection
v1.5.1 — Clean output formatting

EXP-015 v1.0-1.2 — CertificationRecord (canonical exchange format)
  - Immutable graph snapshots (hash-addressed)
  - Observation → Defect pipeline
  - 6 invariant checks (I-1 through I-6)
  - Real data: arXiv:2606.20439 (Ono, Schwartz, Thakur 2026)

ARCHITECTURE
------------
Three independent graphs:
  PDG — Proof Dependency Graph (logical proof structure)
  CDG — Citation Dependency Graph (scholarly attribution)
  EDG — Evidence Dependency Graph (computational artifacts)

Two operation types:
  GraphMutation  — structural change (add edge, citation, evidence)
  StateTransition — obligation state change (MISSING → PROVED_LOCAL, etc.)

Defect taxonomy (10 categories):
  missing_citation, missing_evidence, missing_proof, undefined_notation,
  external_dependency, formalization_gap, conjecture_leak, assumption_leak,
  circular_dependency, orphaned_claim

KEY RESULTS
-----------
CDC 2026:
  PDG exact: True (all logical deps satisfied)
  CDG defect: 4 missing citations
  EDG defect: 2 missing evidence artifacts
  Systematic issue: missing_citation (HIGH)

AQARION v11.4.0:
  PDG exact: True
  CDG defect: 3 missing citations
  EDG defect: 6 missing evidence artifacts
  Systematic issues: missing_evidence (HIGH), missing_citation (HIGH),
                     conjecture_leak (MEDIUM)
  Multi-defect claims: THM-2.8, THM-2.10, THM-2.14, LEM-4.2

arXiv:2606.20439 (Kaprekar):
  27 claims mapped (theorems, lemmas, corollaries, definitions, external refs)
  14 real bibliography entries
  1 confirmed defect: formalization_gap (DEF-001)
    — AxiomProver Lean formalization lacks independent verification
    — Confidence: 0.78, Severity: 0.85
    — Remediation: independent Lean expert review + CI verification
  Invariants: All 6 passed (I-1 through I-6)
