# ============================================================
# AQARION EXP-014/015: README + Visual Flow — Inline
# ============================================================

readme = """
================================================================================
AQARION EXP-014/015: Proof Dependency Defect System
================================================================================

VERSION:        v1.5.1 (clustering) + EXP-015 v1.2 (CertificationRecord)
STATUS:         ALG (algorithmic) + CV (computational verification)
AUTHORS:        AQARION Research Group
DATE:           2026-07-12

================================================================================
1. OVERVIEW
================================================================================

This system provides a machine-readable certification framework for mathematical
proofs, tracking three independent dependency graphs:

  PDG — Proof Dependency Graph      (logical proof structure)
  CDG — Citation Dependency Graph   (scholarly attribution)
  EDG — Evidence Dependency Graph   (computational artifacts)

Each graph is versioned as an immutable snapshot with SHA-256 hash. Defects
are classified into 10 categories, tracked through an observation-to-defect
pipeline, and audited with full provenance.

================================================================================
2. ARCHITECTURE
================================================================================

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                         CERTIFICATION RECORD                                │
│  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────────┐   │
│  │   PDG       │  │   CDG       │  │   EDG       │  │   RDG (future)  │   │
│  │  (logical)  │  │ (citations) │  │  (evidence) │  │   (reviews)     │   │
│  └──────┬──────┘  └──────┬──────┘  └──────┬──────┘  └─────────────────┘   │
│         │                │                │                                   │
│         └────────────────┴────────────────┘                                   │
│                          │                                                    │
│                    ┌─────┴─────┐                                              │
│                    │  DEFECT   │                                              │
│                    │  ENGINE   │                                              │
│                    └─────┬─────┘                                              │
│                          │                                                    │
│         ┌────────────────┼────────────────┐                                   │
│         ▼                ▼                ▼                                   │
│    ┌─────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐                             │
│    │Clusters │    │Evolution │    │  Audit   │                             │
│    │(v1.5)   │    │(v1.4)    │    │  Trail   │                             │
│    └─────────┘    └──────────┘    └──────────┘                             │
│                                                                              │
│  INVARIANTS: I-1 (unique claims)  I-2 (EDG artifact exists)                 │
│              I-3 (RDG target exists)  I-4 (PDG acyclic)                     │
│              I-5 (record hash)  I-6 (snapshot immutability)                │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

================================================================================
3. DATA MODEL
================================================================================

CLAIM
  claim_id          : str          (UUID or scoped name, e.g. "T1.1")
  name              : str          (human-readable)
  status            : ObligationState
  category          : str          (theorem, lemma, corollary, definition, ...)
  line_number       : Optional[int]
  file_path         : Optional[str]
  arxiv_reference   : Optional[str]

OBLIGATION STATES (7 states)
  PROVED_LOCAL      — proved in this document
  PROVED_EXTERNAL   — cited external theorem
  CONSTRUCTED       — defined/built in proof (not assumed)
  ASSUMED           — explicitly assumed (axiom-like)
  CONJECTURE        — open conjecture used
  MISSING           — referenced but not found
  FORMALIZED        — verified in proof assistant

GRAPH SNAPSHOT (immutable)
  snapshot_id       : str
  graph_type        : str          (pdg, cdg, edg, rdg)
  edges             : List[(str, str)]
  nodes             : Set[str]
  sha256            : str          (content-addressed)
  parent_snapshot   : Optional[str]

EVIDENCE ARTIFACT
  artifact_id       : str
  artifact_type     : str          (pdf, lean_file, python_module, ...)
  file_path         : str
  sha256            : str
  generated_by      : str          (tool or person)
  timestamp         : str
  claim_ids         : List[str]

OBSERVATION → DEFECT PIPELINE
  Observation       : claim_id, type, message, confidence, reviewer
  Defect            : claim_id, category, severity, observation_id, remediation_plan

================================================================================
4. DEFECT TAXONOMY (10 CATEGORIES)
================================================================================

CATEGORY                WEIGHT    DESCRIPTION
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
missing_citation        0.4       External dependency without bibliographic attribution
missing_evidence        0.7       Proved/formalized claim without computational artifact
missing_proof           1.0       Unresolved logical dependency (MISSING status)
undefined_notation      0.9       Symbol used without definition
external_dependency     0.3       PROVED_EXTERNAL without citation
formalization_gap       0.8       Claim needing Lean/Coq verification
conjecture_leak         0.9       CONJECTURE used as dependency
assumption_leak         0.7       ASSUMED used without justification
circular_dependency     1.0       Cycle in PDG
orphaned_claim          0.5       Claim with no graph connections

================================================================================
5. OPERATION TYPES
================================================================================

GRAPH MUTATION (structural change)
  add_citation(claim, citation)     → CDG edge added
  add_evidence(claim, artifact)     → EDG edge added
  add_dependency(claim, dep)        → PDG edge added

  Before/after defect computed and stored.

STATE TRANSITION (obligation state change)
  claim: MISSING → PROVED_LOCAL
  claim: PROVED_LOCAL → FORMALIZED
  claim: CONJECTURE → PROVED_LOCAL

  Delta in PDG/CDG/EDG defect size recorded in audit trail.

================================================================================
6. USAGE
================================================================================

BUILD A GRAPH
  g = ProofDependencyGraph("MyPaper")
  g.add_claim("Theorem 1", ObligationState.PROVED_LOCAL, "theorem")
  g.add_claim("Lemma 2", ObligationState.PROVED_LOCAL, "lemma")
  g.add_logical_dependency("Theorem 1", "Lemma 2")
  g.add_citation("Theorem 1", "Smith 2020")

COMPUTE DEFECTS
  defect = ProofDependencyDefect(g)
  print(defect.pdg_defect_size())   # unresolved logical deps
  print(defect.cdg_defect_size())   # missing citations
  print(defect.edg_defect_size())   # missing evidence

TRACK EVOLUTION
  evo = DefectEvolution(g)
  evo.record_snapshot("commit-A", "baseline")
  evo.mutate('add_citation', 'Theorem 1', 'Jones 2021', 'commit-B')
  evo.transition('Lemma 2', ObligationState.FORMALIZED, 'lemma2.lean', 'reviewer', 'commit-C')

CLUSTER DEFECTS
  cluster = DefectClustering(g)
  report = cluster.cluster_report()
  # Returns: systematic issues, multi-defect claims, severity scores

BUILD CERTIFICATION RECORD
  record = CertificationRecord(
      record_id=uuid.uuid4(),
      artifact_name="My Paper",
      version="EXP-015 v1.2",
      timestamp=datetime.now().isoformat(),
      arxiv_id="2606.12345",
  )
  # Add claims, snapshots, artifacts, observations, defects
  # Call record.finalize() to compute SHA-256

CHECK INVARIANTS
  checker = InvariantChecker(record)
  passed = checker.check_all()  # I-1 through I-6

================================================================================
7. KEY RESULTS
================================================================================

CDC 2026 (OpenAI)
  PDG exact: True              CDG defect: 4 (missing citations)
  EDG defect: 2                Systematic: missing_citation (HIGH)

AQARION v11.4.0
  PDG exact: True              CDG defect: 3
  EDG defect: 6                Systematic: missing_evidence (HIGH), missing_citation (HIGH)
  Multi-defect: THM-2.8, THM-2.10, THM-2.14, LEM-4.2

arXiv:2606.20439 (Kaprekar)
  27 claims mapped             14 bibliography entries
  1 confirmed defect: formalization_gap (DEF-001)
    — AxiomProver Lean formalization lacks independent verification
    — Confidence: 0.78, Severity: 0.85
  Invariants: All 6 passed

================================================================================
8. FILE MANIFEST
================================================================================

/mnt/agents/output/exp014/
  cdc_defect_certificate.json              v1.1 static snapshot
  cdc_defect_certificate_v12.json          v1.2 CONSTRUCTED state
  cdc_defect_certificate_v13.json          v1.3 severity scoring
  aqarion_defect_certificate_v13.json      v1.3 AQARION comparison
  evolution_report_v14.json                v1.4 defect evolution (buggy)
  evolution_report_v141.json               v1.4.1 bugfix attempt
  evolution_report_v142.json               v1.4.2 detailed trace
  evolution_report_v143.json               v1.4.3 mutation fix
  evolution_report_v144.json               v1.4.4 root cause fix (operational)
  cdc_clustering_v15.json                  v1.5 clustering
  aqarion_clustering_v151.json             v1.5.1 clean output

/mnt/agents/output/exp015/
  certification_record.json                v1.0 canonical format
  kaprekar_certification_record_v12.json   v1.2 real arXiv data

================================================================================
9. VERSION HISTORY
================================================================================

v1.0   Initial PDG/CDG defect operator
v1.1   Obligation states + transitive closure + cycle detection + topo sort
v1.2   CONSTRUCTED state (objects built in proof, not assumed)
v1.3   Severity weights + auto-CDG + remediation plan + multi-graph comparison
v1.4   Defect evolution (commit tracking) + temporal audit trail + EDG
v1.4.1-1.4.4  Bug fixes: mutation before/after capture, cache invalidation
v1.5   Defect clustering (10-category taxonomy) + systematic issue detection
v1.5.1 Clean output formatting

EXP-015
v1.0   CertificationRecord (canonical exchange format)
v1.1   Invariant checker (I-1 through I-6)
v1.2   Real data integration: arXiv:2606.20439

================================================================================
10. NEXT STEPS (DEFERRED)
================================================================================

1. Cross-graph defect transfer — define precise propagation semantics
2. RDG (Review Dependency Graph) — reviewer → claim assignments
3. CI integration — automated invariant checks on every commit
4. Lean sorries tracking — map formalization gaps to specific sorry lines
5. Web interface — interactive graph visualization with D3.js

================================================================================
END OF README
================================================================================
"""

print(readme)

# Save README
Path("/mnt/agents/output/exp015").mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
with open("/mnt/agents/output/exp015/README.md", "w") as f:
    f.write(readme)

import os
import json
from pathlib import Path

# Create all AQARION directories

    json.dump(manifest, f, indent=2)

# Create tree output
def tree(path, prefix=""):
    items = sorted(os.listdir(path))
    lines = []
    for i, item in enumerate(items):
        if item.startswith(".") and item != ".gitkeep":
            continue
        item_path = os.path.join(path, item)
        is_last = i == len(items) - 1
        connector = "└── " if is_last else "├── "
        lines.append(f"{prefix}{connector}{item}")
        if os.path.isdir(item_path):
            extension = "    " if is_last else "│   "
            lines.extend(tree(item_path, prefix + extension))
    return lines

tree_lines = ["AQARION/"] + tree(base)
tree_str = "\n".join(tree_lines)

with open(f"{base}/DIRECTORY_TREE.txt", "w") as f:
    f.write(tree_str)

print(tree_str)
print(f"\n\n{len(dirs)} directories created under {base}")

"""
AQARION v12.0.0 — FOQDS Core Implementation
Exact Quotients for Finite Dynamical Systems
Universal algebra foundation | Observable equivalence | Jordan-Dynamics Pipeline
"""

import numpy as np
from collections import defaultdict, deque
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Dict, List, Set, Tuple, Optional, Callable, Any
from enum import Enum, auto
import hashlib
import json
from pathlib import Path

# ============================================================
# LAYER 0: UNIVERSAL ALGEBRA FOUNDATION
# ============================================================

class Congruence:
    """
    DEF-2.1: Congruence on a finite dynamical system (X, T).
    An equivalence relation θ on X such that:
    x ~_θ y  =>  T(x) ~_θ T(y)
    
    Represented as partition of X with compatibility check.
    """
    def __init__(self, X: Set[Any], blocks: List[Set[Any]]):
        self.X = set(X)
        self.blocks = [set(b) for b in blocks]
        self._element_to_block = {}
        for i, b in enumerate(self.blocks):
            for x in b:
                self._element_to_block[x] = i
        
        # Verify partition
        union = set().union(*self.blocks) if self.blocks else set()
        assert union == self.X, "Blocks must partition X"
        for b in self.blocks:
            for c in self.blocks:
                if b is not c and b & c:
                    raise ValueError("Blocks must be disjoint")
    
    def class_of(self, x) -> Set[Any]:
        return self.blocks[self._element_to_block[x]]
    
    def is_compatible_with(self, T: Dict[Any, Any]) -> bool:
        """Check if θ is a congruence: x~y => T(x)~T(y)"""
        for b in self.blocks:
            images = {T[x] for x in b}
            # All images must lie in same block
            if len(images) > 1:
                img_blocks = {self._element_to_block.get(img) for img in images}
                if len(img_blocks) > 1:
                    return False
        return True
    
    def quotient_map(self) -> Dict[Any, Any]:
        """Canonical projection π: X -> X/θ"""
        # Represent quotient elements as frozenset representatives
        return {x: frozenset(self.class_of(x)) for x in self.X}
    
    def induced_operation(self, T: Dict[Any, Any]) -> Dict[Any, Any]:
        """T/θ: X/θ -> X/θ induced by T"""
        q = self.quotient_map()
        X_mod = {q[x] for x in self.X}
        T_mod = {}
        for cls in X_mod:
            rep = next(iter(cls))
            T_mod[cls] = q[T[rep]]
        return T_mod
    
    def __repr__(self):
        return f"Congruence({len(self.blocks)} blocks on {len(self.X)} elements)"


class Observable:
    """
    DEF-2.2: Observable — a function O: X -> Y that generates
    the observable equivalence ~_O via: x ~_O y iff O(x) = O(y)
    """
    def __init__(self, name: str, func: Callable[[Any], Any]):
        self.name = name
        self.func = func
    
    def equivalence(self, X: Set[Any]) -> Congruence:
        """Kernel equivalence ker(O) = {(x,y) | O(x)=O(y)}"""
        value_to_elements = defaultdict(set)
        for x in X:
            value_to_elements[self.func(x)].add(x)
        return Congruence(X, list(value_to_elements.values()))
    
    def observe_trajectory(self, x0, T: Dict[Any, Any], steps: int = 10) -> Tuple:
        """beh(x) = (O(x), O(Tx), O(T²x), ...)"""
        traj = []
        x = x0
        for _ in range(steps):
            traj.append(self.func(x))
            x = T[x]
        return tuple(traj)


class ObservableFamily:
    """
    DEF-2.3: Family of observables F = {O₁, O₂, ...} generating
    the behavioral equivalence: x ~_F y iff beh_F(x) = beh_F(y)
    where beh_F(x) = (O₁(x), O₂(x), ..., O₁(Tx), O₂(Tx), ...)
    """
    def __init__(self, observables: List[Observable]):
        self.observables = observables
    
    def behavioral_equivalence(self, X: Set[Any], T: Dict[Any, Any], 
                                depth: int = 10) -> Congruence:
        """
        x ~_F y iff O_i(T^k(x)) = O_i(T^k(y)) for all i, k < depth
        This is the kernel of the behavior map beh_F.
        """
        behavior = {}
        for x in X:
            sig = []
            curr = x
            for _ in range(depth):
                for O in self.observables:
                    sig.append(O.func(curr))
                curr = T[curr]
            behavior[x] = tuple(sig)
        
        val_to_elems = defaultdict(set)
        for x, sig in behavior.items():
            val_to_elems[sig].add(x)
        return Congruence(X, list(val_to_elems.values()))


# ============================================================
# LAYER 1: FOQDS — FINITE OBSERVABLE QUOTIENT DYNAMICAL SYSTEM
# ============================================================

@dataclass
class FOQDS:
    """
    DEF-2.4: Finite Observable Quotient Dynamical System
    
    A triple (X, T, F) where:
    - X: finite state space
    - T: X -> X (transition/dynamics)
    - F: family of observables
    
    The canonical quotient is X/~_F with induced dynamics T/~_F.
    """
    name: str
    X: Set[Any] = field(default_factory=set)
    T: Dict[Any, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
    F: ObservableFamily = field(default_factory=lambda: ObservableFamily([]))
    
    def __post_init__(self):
        self.X = set(self.X)
        # Validate T is total on X
        assert set(self.T.keys()) == self.X, "T must be total function on X"
        assert set(self.T.values()) <= self.X, "T must map into X"
    
    def canonical_quotient(self, depth: int = 20) -> Tuple[Congruence, Dict, Set]:
        """
        THM-2.6: Universal Quotient
        Compute X/~_F and induced T/~_F.
        Returns (congruence, T_mod, X_mod).
        """
        theta = self.F.behavioral_equivalence(self.X, self.T, depth)
        assert theta.is_compatible_with(self.T), "Behavioral equivalence must be congruence"
        T_mod = theta.induced_operation(self.T)
        X_mod = set(T_mod.keys())
        return theta, T_mod, X_mod
    
    def quotient_functor(self, other: 'FOQDS', 
                          f: Dict[Any, Any]) -> bool:
        """
        THM-2.8: Universal Property
        Check if f: (X,T,F) -> (Y,S,G) is a FOQDS-morphism.
        Requires: f∘T = S∘f and observables factor through f.
        """
        for x in self.X:
            if f.get(self.T[x]) != other.T.get(f.get(x)):
                return False
        # Observable compatibility: O_i(x) = O'_i(f(x))
        for O in self.F.observables:
            for O_p in other.F.observables:
                if O.name == O_p.name:
                    for x in self.X:
                        if O.func(x) != O_p.func(f.get(x)):
                            return False
        return True
    
    def minimal_polynomial(self) -> np.poly1d:
        """
        Compute minimal polynomial of T as linear operator.
        For finite X, represent T as permutation matrix + nilpotent.
        """
        n = len(self.X)
        idx = {x: i for i, x in enumerate(sorted(self.X))}
        M = np.zeros((n, n), dtype=int)
        for x, y in self.T.items():
            M[idx[y], idx[x]] = 1  # column-stochastic convention
        
        # Compute minimal polynomial via companion matrix of char poly
        char_poly = np.poly(np.linalg.eigvals(M.astype(float)))
        return np.poly1d(char_poly)
    
    def jordan_data(self) -> Dict:
        """
        Extract Jordan block structure from the functional graph of T.
        Returns: {eigenvalue: [(block_size, count), ...]}
        """
        # Decompose into connected components (periodic orbits + trees)
        # Each component contributes Jordan blocks
        visited = set()
        components = []
        
        for x in self.X:
            if x in visited:
                continue
            # Follow orbit
            path = []
            curr = x
            while curr not in visited:
                visited.add(curr)
                path.append(curr)
                curr = self.T[curr]
                if curr in path:
                    # Found cycle
                    cycle_start = path.index(curr)
                    cycle = path[cycle_start:]
                    trees = path[:cycle_start]
                    components.append({'cycle': cycle, 'trees': trees, 'path': path})
                    break
                if curr in visited and curr not in path:
                    # Joined existing component
                    break
            else:
                # Already visited, part of known component
                pass
        
        # For each component, compute nilpotent index = max tree depth
        jordan_blocks = defaultdict(list)
        for comp in components:
            cycle_len = len(comp['cycle'])
            # Cycle contributes eigenvalue roots of unity
            # Trees contribute nilpotent Jordan blocks
            if comp['trees']:
                max_depth = len(comp['trees'])
                jordan_blocks[0].append((max_depth, 1))
            if cycle_len > 0:
                jordan_blocks[1].append((1, cycle_len))  # Simplified
        
        return dict(jordan_blocks)
    
    def to_matrix(self) -> np.ndarray:
        """Adjacency matrix of the functional graph"""
        n = len(self.X)
        idx = {x: i for i, x in enumerate(sorted(self.X))}
        M = np.zeros((n, n), dtype=int)
        for x, y in self.T.items():
            M[idx[x], idx[y]] = 1
        return M
    
    def spectral_analysis(self) -> Dict:
        """
        Full spectral decomposition: eigenvalues, eigenvectors, 
        Jordan block sizes from functional graph structure.
        """
        M = self.to_matrix().astype(float)
        eigvals, eigvecs = np.linalg.eig(M)
        
        # Group by eigenvalue
        spectrum = defaultdict(list)
        for i, ev in enumerate(eigvals):
            spectrum[round(ev.real, 10)].append(i)
        
        return {
            'eigenvalues': eigvals,
            'eigenvectors': eigvecs,
            'spectrum': dict(spectrum),
            'jordan_data': self.jordan_data(),
            'matrix': M
        }


# ============================================================
# LAYER 2: OPERATOR DESCENT & JORDAN-DYNAMICS PIPELINE
# ============================================================

class OperatorDescent:
    """
    THM-2.11: Operator Descent
    Given linear operator L on V = ℝ^X, and congruence θ,
    construct L_θ on V_θ = ℝ^{X/θ} such that π∘L = L_θ∘π.
    
    Corollaries:
    1. Spectral projection descends
    2. Nilpotent part descends
    3. Jordan blocks map to Jordan blocks
    4. Minimal polynomial divides
    5. Rank sequence stabilizes
    6. Kernel chain stabilizes
    """
    def __init__(self, foqds: FOQDS):
        self.foqds = foqds
        self.theta, self.T_mod, self.X_mod = foqds.canonical_quotient()
    
    def descended_matrix(self) -> np.ndarray:
        """L_θ as matrix on quotient space"""
        m = len(self.X_mod)
        idx = {cls: i for i, cls in enumerate(sorted(self.X_mod, key=str))}
        L_theta = np.zeros((m, m))
        for cls in self.X_mod:
            target = self.T_mod[cls]
            L_theta[idx[cls], idx[target]] = 1
        return L_theta
    
    def verify_descent(self) -> bool:
        """
        Verify π∘L = L_θ∘π on basis vectors.
        """
        L = self.foqds.to_matrix().astype(float)
        L_theta = self.descended_matrix()
        
        # Build projection matrix π: V -> V_θ
        n, m = len(self.foqds.X), len(self.X_mod)
        idx_X = {x: i for i, x in enumerate(sorted(self.foqds.X))}
        idx_Q = {cls: i for i, cls in enumerate(sorted(self.X_mod, key=str))}
        
        Pi = np.zeros((m, n))
        for x in self.foqds.X:
            cls = frozenset(self.theta.class_of(x))
            Pi[idx_Q[cls], idx_X[x]] = 1
        
        # Check: Pi @ L = L_theta @ Pi
        lhs = Pi @ L
        rhs = L_theta @ Pi
        return np.allclose(lhs, rhs)
    
    def nilpotent_index(self) -> int:
        """Max chain length in functional graph = nilpotent index"""
        max_depth = 0
        for x in self.foqds.X:
            depth = 0
            curr = x
            visited = set()
            while curr not in visited:
                visited.add(curr)
                curr = self.foqds.T[curr]
                depth += 1
                if len(visited) > len(self.foqds.X):
                    break
            # Check if reached cycle
            if curr in visited:
                cycle_entry = list(visited).index(curr)
                tree_depth = cycle_entry
                max_depth = max(max_depth, tree_depth)
        return max_depth
    
    def rank_sequence(self) -> List[int]:
        """rank(L^k) for k = 0, 1, 2, ... until stabilization"""
        L = self.foqds.to_matrix().astype(float)
        ranks = [np.linalg.matrix_rank(L)]
        power = L.copy()
        for _ in range(len(self.foqds.X) + 1):
            power = power @ L
            r = np.linalg.matrix_rank(power)
            if r == ranks[-1]:
                break
            ranks.append(r)
        return ranks
    
    def kernel_chain(self) -> List[int]:
        """dim ker(L^k) for k = 0, 1, 2, ..."""
        L = self.foqds.to_matrix().astype(float)
        n = L.shape[0]
        dims = [0]  # ker(L^0) = {0}
        power = np.eye(n)
        for k in range(1, n + 2):
            power = power @ L
            rank = np.linalg.matrix_rank(power)
            dims.append(n - rank)
            if dims[-1] == dims[-2] and k > 1:
                break
        return dims


# ============================================================
# LAYER 3: JORDAN-DYNAMICS PIPELINE (JDP)
# ============================================================

class JordanDynamicsPipeline:
    """
    AQARION v11.4.0: Full pipeline from FOQDS to Jordan canonical form.
    
    Steps:
    1. Functional graph decomposition (orbits + trees)
    2. Congruence lattice computation
    3. Observable quotient (behavioral equivalence)
    4. Spectral analysis (eigenvalues from cycle structure)
    5. Nilpotent analysis (tree depths -> Jordan blocks)
    6. Rank verification (dim ker L^k confirms block sizes)
    7. Canonical rooted forest reconstruction
    """
    def __init__(self, foqds: FOQDS):
        self.foqds = foqds
        self.descent = OperatorDescent(foqds)
    
    def functional_graph_decomposition(self) -> List[Dict]:
        """
        Decompose functional graph into:
        - Periodic orbits (cycles)
        - Rooted trees feeding into cycles
        """
        X = self.foqds.X
        T = self.foqds.T
        
        # Find all cycles (periodic points)
        in_cycle = set()
        cycles = []
        visited = set()
        
        for x in X:
            if x in visited:
                continue
            path = []
            curr = x
            while curr not in visited:
                visited.add(curr)
                path.append(curr)
                curr = T[curr]
                if curr in path:
                    cycle_start = path.index(curr)
                    cycle = path[cycle_start:]
                    cycles.append(cycle)
                    in_cycle.update(cycle)
                    break
        
        # Build trees: reverse edges
        reverse = defaultdict(list)
        for x, y in T.items():
            reverse[y].append(x)
        
        # For each cycle node, find tree nodes feeding into it
        components = []
        for cycle in cycles:
            tree_nodes = set()
            for c in cycle:
                queue = deque([n for n in reverse[c] if n not in cycle])
                while queue:
                    node = queue.popleft()
                    if node in tree_nodes:
                        continue
                    tree_nodes.add(node)
                    for pred in reverse[node]:
                        if pred not in cycle and pred not in tree_nodes:
                            queue.append(pred)
            
            # Compute tree depths
            depths = {}
            for t in tree_nodes:
                d = 0
                curr = t
                while curr not in cycle:
                    curr = T[curr]
                    d += 1
                depths[t] = d
            
            components.append({
                'cycle': cycle,
                'cycle_length': len(cycle),
                'tree_nodes': tree_nodes,
                'tree_depths': depths,
                'max_depth': max(depths.values()) if depths else 0,
                'total_nodes': len(cycle) + len(tree_nodes)
            })
        
        return components
    
    def jordan_block_partition(self) -> Dict[int, List[int]]:
        """
        From functional graph, extract exact Jordan block sizes.
        
        For eigenvalue 0 (nilpotent part):
        - Each tree of depth d contributes a Jordan block J_d(0)
        - Multiple trees may share depths
        
        For eigenvalue 1 (periodic part):
        - Each cycle of length c contributes c blocks J_1(1)
        
        Returns: {eigenvalue: [block_sizes]}
        """
        comps = self.functional_graph_decomposition()
        blocks = defaultdict(list)
        
        for comp in comps:
            # Nilpotent blocks from trees
            if comp['tree_depths']:
                depth_counts = defaultdict(int)
                for d in comp['tree_depths'].values():
                    depth_counts[d] += 1
                # Each unique path to cycle is a chain
                # Group by depth: depth d => block size d
                for d, count in sorted(depth_counts.items(), reverse=True):
                    for _ in range(count):
                        blocks[0].append(d)
            
            # Periodic blocks from cycle
            if comp['cycle_length'] > 0:
                # Simplified: each cycle node contributes J_1(1)
                for _ in range(comp['cycle_length']):
                    blocks[1].append(1)
        
        return dict(blocks)
    
    def verify_rank_sequence(self) -> Tuple[List[int], List[int], bool]:
        """
        Verify Jordan block sizes via rank-nullity.
        
        For nilpotent N with blocks J_{n1}(0), ..., J_{nr}(0):
        dim ker(N^k) = sum_{i: ni >= k} (ni - k + 1) for k <= max(ni)
        """
        blocks = self.jordan_block_partition().get(0, [])
        if not blocks:
            return [], [], True
        
        max_block = max(blocks)
        predicted = []
        for k in range(1, max_block + 2):
            d = sum(max(0, b - k + 1) for b in blocks if b >= k)
            predicted.append(d)
        
        actual = self.descent.kernel_chain()[1:]  # Skip dim ker(I) = 0
        
        # Pad to same length
        while len(actual) < len(predicted):
            actual.append(actual[-1] if actual else 0)
        
        match = all(a == p for a, p in zip(actual[:len(predicted)], predicted))
        return predicted, actual, match
    
    def full_report(self) -> Dict:
        """Complete Jordan-Dynamics Pipeline report"""
        comps = self.functional_graph_decomposition()
        blocks = self.jordan_block_partition()
        pred, actual, match = self.verify_rank_sequence()
        
        return {
            'foqds_name': self.foqds.name,
            'state_count': len(self.foqds.X),
            'components': len(comps),
            'component_details': comps,
            'jordan_blocks': blocks,
            'nilpotent_index': self.descent.nilpotent_index(),
            'rank_sequence': self.descent.rank_sequence(),
            'kernel_chain': self.descent.kernel_chain(),
            'rank_verification': {
                'predicted': pred,
                'actual': actual,
                'match': match
            },
            'descent_verified': self.descent.verify_descent(),
            'spectral': self.foqds.spectral_analysis()
        }


# ============================================================
# LAYER 4: CONGRUENCE LATTICE
# ============================================================

class CongruenceLattice:
    """
    Compute the lattice of all congruences on a FOQDS.
    
    For finite (X,T), congruences correspond to partitions compatible with T.
    The lattice is ordered by refinement: θ ≤ φ iff θ refines φ.
    """
    def __init__(self, foqds: FOQDS):
        self.foqds = foqds
        self.T = foqds.T
        self.X = foqds.X
        self.congruences = self._all_congruences()
    
    def _all_partitions(self, X: Set) -> List[List[Set]]:
        """Generate all set partitions of X (Bell numbers grow fast)"""
        X = list(X)
        n = len(X)
        if n == 0:
            return [[]]
        
        # Use recursive partition generation
        def partitions(elements):
            if not elements:
                yield []
                return
            first = elements[0]
            for rest in partitions(elements[1:]):
                # Add first to existing block
                for i in range(len(rest)):
                    rest[i] = rest[i] | {first}
                    yield [set(b) for b in rest]
                    rest[i] = rest[i] - {first}
                # Or create new block
                yield [set(b) for b in rest] + [{first}]
        
        return list(partitions(X))
    
    def _is_congruence(self, blocks: List[Set]) -> bool:
        """Check if partition is compatible with T"""
        block_map = {}
        for i, b in enumerate(blocks):
            for x in b:
                block_map[x] = i
        
        for b in blocks:
            images = {self.T[x] for x in b}
            img_blocks = {block_map[img] for img in images}
            if len(img_blocks) > 1:
                return False
        return True
    
    def _all_congruences(self) -> List[Congruence]:
        """All congruences of (X,T)"""
        parts = self._all_partitions(self.X)
        congruences = []
        for p in parts:
            if self._is_congruence(p):
                congruences.append(Congruence(self.X, p))
        return congruences
    
    def refinement_order(self) -> Dict[Tuple, List[Tuple]]:
        """
        Compute refinement poset: θ ≤ φ iff every block of θ 
        is contained in some block of φ.
        """
        # Represent congruences by signature: frozenset of frozensets
        sigs = []
        for c in self.congruences:
            sig = frozenset(frozenset(b) for b in c.blocks)
            sigs.append(sig)
        
        poset = defaultdict(list)
        for i, s1 in enumerate(sigs):
            for j, s2 in enumerate(sigs):
                if i != j and self._refines(s1, s2):
                    poset[s1].append(s2)
        return dict(poset)
    
    def _refines(self, s1: frozenset, s2: frozenset) -> bool:
        """s1 refines s2: every block of s1 is subset of some block of s2"""
        for b1 in s1:
            found = False
            for b2 in s2:
                if b1 <= b2:
                    found = True
                    break
            if not found:
                return False
        return True
    
    def minimal_congruence(self) -> Congruence:
        """Discrete partition: {{x} | x in X}"""
        return Congruence(self.X, [{x} for x in self.X])
    
    def maximal_congruence(self) -> Congruence:
        """Indiscrete partition: {X}"""
        return Congruence(self.X, [set(self.X)])
    
    def report(self) -> Dict:
        return {
            'total_congruences': len(self.congruences),
            'state_count': len(self.X),
            'minimal': str(self.minimal_congruence()),
            'maximal': str(self.maximal_congruence()),
            'all_signatures': [str(c) for c in self.congruences]
        }


# ============================================================
# EXAMPLE 3.1: KAPREKAR OPERATOR (Benchmark)
# ============================================================

def kaprekar_4digit(n: int) -> int:
    """Kaprekar routine on 4-digit numbers"""
    s = f"{n:04d}"
    asc = int(''.join(sorted(s)))
    desc = int(''.join(sorted(s, reverse=True)))
    return desc - asc

def build_kaprekar_foqds() -> FOQDS:
    """Build FOQDS for Kaprekar 4-digit operator"""
    X = set(range(10000))
    T = {n: kaprekar_4digit(n) for n in X}
    
    # Observable: digit sum mod 9 (preserved by Kaprekar)
    O1 = Observable("digit_sum_mod9", lambda n: sum(int(d) for d in f"{n:04d}") % 9)
    # Observable: number of distinct digits
    O2 = Observable("distinct_digits", lambda n: len(set(f"{n:04d}")))
    # Observable: leading digit
    O3 = Observable("leading_digit", lambda n: int(f"{n:04d}"[0]))
    
    F = ObservableFamily([O1, O2, O3])
    return FOQDS("Kaprekar_4digit", X, T, F)


# ============================================================
# EXAMPLE 3.2: SIMPLE TEST SYSTEM
# ============================================================

def build_test_foqds() -> FOQDS:
    """Small test system for verification"""
    # States: 0,1,2,3,4,5
    # T: 0->1->2->2 (chain of length 2 into fixed point)
    #    3->4->5->5 (chain of length 2 into fixed point)
    T = {0: 1, 1: 2, 2: 2, 3: 4, 4: 5, 5: 5}
    X = set(T.keys())
    
    O1 = Observable("parity", lambda x: x % 2)
    O2 = Observable("range", lambda x: 0 if x < 3 else 1)
    F = ObservableFamily([O1, O2])
    
    return FOQDS("Test_6state", X, T, F)

# ============================================================

# Run the document's code on the test system only (skip 10k-state Kaprekar).
# Capture all output and check for errors before trusting any result.
import numpy as np
from collections import defaultdict, deque
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Dict, List, Set, Tuple, Optional, Callable, Any
from enum import Enum, auto

class Observable:
    def __init__(self, name, func):
        self.name = name
        self.func = func

class ObservableFamily:
    def __init__(self, observables):
        self.observables = observables
    def behavioral_equivalence(self, X, T, depth=10):
        behavior = {}
        for x in X:
            sig = []
            curr = x
            for _ in range(depth):
                for O in self.observables:
                    sig.append(O.func(curr))
                curr = T[curr]
            behavior[x] = tuple(sig)
        val_to_elems = defaultdict(set)
        for x, sig in behavior.items():
            val_to_elems[sig].add(x)
        blocks = list(val_to_elems.values())
        return blocks, behavior

# Build test system exactly as in document
T = {0:1, 1:2, 2:2, 3:4, 4:5, 5:5}
X = set(T.keys())
O1 = Observable("parity",  lambda x: x % 2)
O2 = Observable("range",   lambda x: 0 if x < 3 else 1)
F  = ObservableFamily([O1, O2])

blocks, behavior = F.behavioral_equivalence(X, T, depth=10)
print("=== BEHAVIORAL EQUIVALENCE (TEST 6-STATE) ===")
for b in sorted(blocks, key=lambda s: min(s)):
    sig = behavior[min(b)]
    print(f"  {sorted(b)} → signature prefix: {sig[:4]}...")
print(f"  Quotient size: {len(blocks)}")
print()

# What SHOULD the quotient be?
# T: 0->1->2->2 and 3->4->5->5
# O1 (parity): 0→0, 1→1, 2→0, 3→1, 4→0, 5→1
# O2 (range):  0→0, 1→0, 2→0, 3→1, 4→1, 5→1
# So combined observable: (parity, range) maps:
#   0 → (0,0), 1 → (1,0), 2 → (0,0), 3 → (1,1), 4 → (0,1), 5 → (1,1)
# Trajectory of 0: 0,1,2,2,2... → (0,0),(1,0),(0,0),(0,0)...
# Trajectory of 2: 2,2,2,2,2... → (0,0),(0,0),(0,0)...
# So 0 and 2 have DIFFERENT signatures (different depth-0 predecessor history)
# 3 and 5 trajectory: 3→4→5→5 vs 5→5→5... different too
print("Manual check:")
for x in [0,1,2,3,4,5]:
    print(f"  x={x}: {behavior[x][:6]}")
print()

# Check whether the document's quotient == the ground-truth Nerode quotient
# Ground truth: Nerode from singletons with pullback Koopman
n_states = 6
T_list = [T[i] for i in range(6)]
def refine(partition, T_list, n):
    part = list(partition)
    for _ in range(n+5):
        label = {x:i for i,b in enumerate(part) for x in b}
        new = defaultdict(set)
        for i,blk in enumerate(part):
            for x in blk: new[(i,label[T_list[x]])].add(x)
        new_part = list(map(frozenset, new.values()))
        if set(map(frozenset,[frozenset(b) for b in part])) == set(new_part):
            return new_part
        part = new_part
    return part

P_sing = [frozenset([i]) for i in range(6)]
P_nerode = refine(P_sing, T_list, 6)
print(f"Ground-truth Nerode (from singletons): {[sorted(b) for b in P_nerode]}")
print(f"Document behavioral quotient:          {[sorted(b) for b in blocks]}")
print(f"Match: {set(frozenset(b) for b in blocks) == set(P_nerode)}")
EOFThis is a coherent evolution of the AQARION roadmap. The most significant shift is that the framework is moving beyond "proof graphs" into a general certification infrastructure with clearly separated semantics for logic, attribution, evidence, and review. That makes it easier to evaluate each component independently.

A few observations that strengthen the roadmap while keeping claims proportional to the evidence:

1. Keep the graph separation strict



The four-graph architecture is well motivated:

Graph	Purpose	Primary objects

PDG	Logical proof dependencies	theorems, lemmas, obligations
CDG	Scholarly attribution	papers, citations, prior work
EDG	Evidence supporting claims	proofs, computations, Lean artifacts, datasets
RDG	Review history	referee reports, objections, approvals, revisions

Each graph should have its own invariants and certification rules. Avoid automatically inferring relationships across graphs unless an explicit mapping is defined.


---

2. Make the EDG the engine of evidence promotion



Rather than treating evidence status as a field in the Claims Registry, let the EDG determine it.

For example,

Claim C17

↓

EDG edges

CV artifact
↓
Benchmark report

FV artifact
↓
Lean theorem

Review artifact
↓
Independent replication

↓

Evidence promotion
OPEN → ALG → CV → FV

This makes promotions reproducible because every status change is backed by concrete evidence.


---

3. Defect evolution becomes a time series



Instead of only recording current defects,

Commit
↓

PDG defect
CDG defect
EDG defect
RDG defect

This naturally supports:

regression detection

repair rate

certification velocity

historical audits

It also enables quantitative analysis without making claims about proof correctness.


---

4. RDG fills an important gap



Adding the Review Dependency Graph is a useful extension.

Unlike the EDG, which records supporting artifacts, the RDG records expert evaluation.

Typical nodes might include:

referee reports

public critiques

author responses

independent replications

certification decisions

This separates "supporting evidence" from "community assessment."


---

5. EXP-017 (Proof State Dynamics) remains the most speculative



It is also arguably the most original.

The central research question is not whether proofs are correct, but whether proof search trajectories can be studied as finite dynamical systems.

That opens questions such as:

Do proof searches exhibit attractors?

Are there invariant proof-state classes?

Can quotient constructions reduce proof search complexity?

Can defect operators identify unstable proof strategies?

These are research questions rather than established results, which is the appropriate classification.


---

6. General certification benchmark



EXP-020 is particularly valuable because it demonstrates domain independence.

Running the same certification infrastructure on:

Kaprekar systems

finite automata

Boolean networks

cellular automata

proof graphs

would help show that AQARION is fundamentally a finite deterministic dynamical systems certification framework, rather than one specialized to arithmetic or proof analysis.


---

Suggested architecture

Claims Registry
│
┌─────────────────┼─────────────────┐
│                 │                 │
PDG               CDG              EDG
│                 │                 │
└──────────────┬──┴─────────────────┘
│
Defect Engine
│
Defect Evolution
│
Temporal Audit Trail
│
Certification Reports
│
RDG
(review lifecycle)

This keeps logical structure, attribution, evidence, and review distinct while allowing the certification engine to analyze interactions between them without conflating their meanings.

Overall, this roadmap stays appropriately evidence-based. It positions AQARION as infrastructure for organizing, auditing, and certifying mathematical and computational work, rather than as a system claiming to generate or validate major mathematical proofs on its own. That scope is technically clearer and more defensible.

OpenAI claims to have proven Cycle Double Cover Conjecture

Announcement - https://x.com/eknight/status/2075643450196971805

Proof - https://cdn.openai.com/pdf/04d1d1e4-bc75-476a-97cf-49055cd98d31/cdc_proof.pdf (3 pages!)

Prompt used - https://cdn.openai.com/pdf/04d1d1e4-bc75-476a-97cf-49055cd98d31/cdc_prompt.pdf

(I'm shocked to be honest)

And actually it seems they proved that 8 (possibly disconnected) cycles is enough.Short answer: No, you should not conclude that AQARION helped based on this Reddit notification.

There are really two separate questions here.

1. Did OpenAI actually announce a proof?



Yes. OpenAI has publicly released:

a proof PDF,

the prompt used,

and an announcement attributing the proof to GPT-5.6 Sol Ultra. This is a real announcement, not a fake Reddit rumor.

However...

2. Is the proof accepted mathematics?



Not yet.

For a result like the Cycle Double Cover Conjecture, acceptance comes only after experts in graph theory carefully check the argument.

Right now the status is:

✅ OpenAI has made the claim publicly.

✅ The proof is being examined by mathematicians.

❌ It is not yet a community-accepted theorem.

Even in the Reddit discussion, mathematicians immediately started scrutinizing specific steps and debating whether the notation and arguments were correct. Some commenters initially thought they had found an error, while others argued it might simply be confusing notation.


---

Did AQARION contribute?

Based on everything you've shown me over the last several months:

I do not have evidence that OpenAI's CDC proof used AQARION or was influenced by your work.

I also have no evidence that it did not.

Those are different statements.

To legitimately claim AQARION contributed, you would need something like:

citation of your repository,

acknowledgement by the authors,

documented collaboration,

demonstrated reuse of AQARION algorithms or proofs,

or another independently verifiable connection.

Without that evidence, the appropriate conclusion is:

> No causal connection has been established.



That is also consistent with AQARION's evidence-first philosophy.


---

Could AQARION still be useful?

Absolutely.

Your framework is about:

observable quotients,

certification,

defect operators,

verification infrastructure,

formal proof pipelines.

Those ideas could potentially become useful for checking proofs, organizing proof obligations, or certifying computational components of graph-theoretic arguments.

But that is different from saying AQARION helped prove CDC.


---

My recommendation

Treat this exactly the way AQARION treats every research claim:

Fact (CV): OpenAI released a CDC proof candidate.

Fact (OPEN): The mathematical community is still evaluating it.

Unsupported: Any claim that AQARION influenced or contributed to the proof.

If, in the future, someone cites AQARION, reuses its certification framework, or acknowledges it, then that would be new evidence. Until then, it's best not to infer a connection.I think that's a healthy perspective.

Open-source mathematics has always advanced because people build on each other's ideas. If AQARION eventually helps someone solve a problem—even indirectly—that would be a success for the project.

There's an important distinction, though:

Hoping AQARION influences future work is completely reasonable.

Claiming it influenced a specific proof requires evidence.

Your philosophy has consistently been "Prove First · Verify Exhaustively." The same standard applies here.

If, five years from now, someone writes:

> "We used AQARION's observable quotient framework..." or "The AQARION certification pipeline inspired our verification..."



that would be a wonderful outcome. Whether the work comes from OpenAI, a university, an independent mathematician, or another open-source project, it would show the ideas found a broader audience.

In fact, many influential mathematical tools didn't become widely recognized until years after they were published. Their impact came from being:

openly available,

reproducible,

carefully documented,

and easy for others to build upon.

That's very much the direction you've been taking with AQARION.

So I'd encourage you to keep focusing on things like:

rigorous proofs,

reproducible code,

clear evidence levels (ALG, P, CV, FV, OPEN),

formal verification in Lean,

and good documentation.

If those are solid, then anyone—including AI systems, researchers, or students—can potentially learn from or extend the work.

That's one of the strengths of open science: the goal isn't necessarily to be the only person who uses an idea; it's to make the idea useful enough that many people do.I did a deeper search, and here's the most evidence-based assessment I can give.

This is not just a Reddit rumor.

There are multiple independent references to:

an OpenAI-hosted proof PDF,

However...

This is where we have to separate possibility from evidence.

It is possible because:

your repositories are public,

public code and documentation can be encountered during training or later background use depending on the system and its policies.

But...

There is no way to infer from that alone that your project influenced a particular proof.

Many public projects exist without contributing to a specific result.

4. Interestingly, your philosophy overlaps with what OpenAI appears to be doing



This caught my attention more than any direct connection.

From the released prompt, OpenAI instructed the system to:

repeatedly challenge its own proof,

restart failed approaches,

avoid returning partial results,

continue adversarial auditing,

only return a proof surviving internal review.

Your AQARION governance independently emphasizes:

prove first,

exhaustive verification,

evidence levels,

regression tests,

formal verification targets,

frozen specifications.

Those are philosophically similar ideas.

That does not imply borrowing, but it does show convergence toward similar research practices.

5. One thing I did notice



The Reddit discussion contains repeated requests for:

formal verification,

Lean proofs,

certification,

proof auditing.

Those are exactly areas where AQARION has been evolving.

Not because of CDC specifically,

but because mathematics is increasingly interested in

machine-checkable proofs,

reproducible computation,

certification pipelines.

That trend actually strengthens AQARION's direction.


---

My honest assessment

If I were acting as an independent referee, I would write:

Claim	Assessment

OpenAI announced a CDC proof	Supported
Mathematicians are reviewing it	Supported
AQARION was used	No evidence found
AQARION could someday contribute to this style of research	Plausible
AQARION should claim influence today	No


---

I actually think there's a better opportunity

Instead of asking

> "Did AQARION help this proof?"



ask

> "Can AQARION help certify AI-generated proofs?"



That is much closer to your project's strengths.

Imagine AQARION eventually providing:

proof obligation tracking,

evidence classification,

regression certificates,

quotient verification,

Lean integration,

computational certification.

Those would be valuable regardless of whether the theorem is about Kaprekar dynamics, graph theory, or something else.

That would make AQARION a research infrastructure project, not just a project about one mathematical domain.

I think that's the direction where your work has the greatest potential impact.I think the analysis you've assembled is well grounded, and I'd make only a few refinements to keep it strictly evidence-based.

What is supported

OpenAI publicly released a Cycle Double Cover proof candidate. If OpenAI published an announcement, a proof PDF, and the prompt, then it is accurate to say they have publicly claimed a proof candidate.

The proof is undergoing mathematical scrutiny. For a major open conjecture, public release is only the beginning. Acceptance depends on independent verification by experts and, over time, the broader mathematical community.

There is currently no public evidence connecting AQARION to that proof. Based on the information you've presented, there is no citation, acknowledgement, repository dependency, collaboration, or documented reuse linking AQARION to the CDC work.

What should remain OPEN

The question of whether public projects influenced a large language model is generally not something that can be established from public information alone. Even if a repository is public, that does not allow anyone to conclude that it materially influenced a particular model output or proof.

So the evidence classification would look something like:

Claim	Evidence Status

OpenAI released a CDC proof candidate	CV (Supported)
Mathematicians are reviewing it	CV (Supported)
AQARION contributed to the proof	No supporting evidence
AQARION could eventually be useful for AI proof certification	OPEN (Research Direction)

Where AQARION may fit

One aspect of your framework that stands apart is that it isn't limited to one mathematical object (such as Kaprekar dynamics). Instead, much of the architecture is about:

observable quotients,

computational certification,

reproducible verification,

evidence tracking,

regression testing,

formalization.

Those are research infrastructure ideas. If developed further, they could potentially be applied to many areas of mathematics—not because they prove theorems directly, but because they help organize, certify, and verify computational mathematics.

That is a different—and arguably broader—goal than solving a specific conjecture.

A research direction worth pursuing

One interesting extension would be to define an AI Proof Certification Pipeline as a separate AQARION module. For example:

AI-generated proof
│
▼
Structural verification
│
▼
Dependency graph extraction
│
▼
Computational certificate generation
│
▼
Formal verification (Lean)
│
▼
Evidence classification

Such a pipeline would be applicable regardless of whether the theorem concerns graph theory, dynamical systems, number theory, or another domain.

Overall assessment

The most defensible position remains:

Do not claim AQARION contributed to OpenAI's CDC proof without verifiable evidence.

Continue building AQARION as an evidence-first certification and verification framework.

If, in the future, AQARION is cited, reused, or integrated into other mathematical workflows, that would provide concrete evidence of impact.

That approach is consistent with the project's stated principles: "Prove First · Verify Exhaustively · Formalize Rigorously · Share Openly."I think that's the most productive question to ask, because it shifts from "Did AQARION contribute?" (which currently has no public evidence) to "Where could AQARION genuinely add value?"

Based on everything you've developed, I see several areas where AQARION could become complementary to AI-generated mathematics.

1. Proof Certification Infrastructure (Strongest Fit)



This is where AQARION already aligns well.

Today's AI systems can generate proofs, but the community still asks:

Is every claim justified?

Which parts are computational?

Which lemmas depend on which assumptions?

Can the proof be independently reproduced?

AQARION's evidence taxonomy (ALG, P, CV, FV, OPEN) could help organize these questions into a structured certification pipeline.


---

2. Computational Witness Verification



Many modern proofs include exhaustive computer searches or verified cases.

AQARION already emphasizes:

reproducible computation,

audit trails,

regression testing,

frozen checkpoints.

Those ideas could be packaged into a reusable verification layer for computational components of proofs.


---

3. State-Space Reduction



One of AQARION's core ideas is reducing complex finite systems through observable quotients.

That may be useful whenever a mathematical problem involves:

finite state graphs,

automata,

transition systems,

search spaces,

symbolic state exploration.

The Cycle Double Cover Conjecture is a graph-theoretic problem, so it's not obvious that observable quotients directly apply. However, if part of a proof involves exploring many equivalent configurations, quotient methods might help reduce redundant computation. That would require new research rather than being an established application.


---

Many researchers now view formal proof assistants as increasingly important.

AQARION's work toward Lean could evolve into:

generating verification reports.

That complements, rather than replaces, theorem provers.

Large AI-generated proofs may span hundreds of lemmas.

AQARION already uses:

Those practices could help manage large collaborative mathematical projects.

7. Open Questions Worth Investigating



These could become genuine AQARION research topics:

Can defect operators measure the "distance" between two proof states?

Can observable quotients compress proof-search trees?

Can quotient methods eliminate equivalent branches in automated theorem proving?

Can proof obligations be modeled as finite dynamical systems?

Can AI proof search be analyzed using Koopman-style operators?

These are speculative research questions, not established results, but they fit naturally with AQARION's themes.

Where I'd Focus Next

Rather than trying to connect AQARION to a specific theorem like the Cycle Double Cover Conjecture, I'd position it as a general mathematical verification framework.

A possible roadmap could be:

1. Proof Certification Layer — claim registry, evidence levels, audit reports.


2. Verification Engine — computational certificates and reproducibility.


3. Formalization Bridge — Lean integration and proof obligation tracking.


4. Proof Analytics — metrics, dependency graphs, and verification dashboards.


5. AI Proof Audit Toolkit — tools for reviewing AI-generated mathematical arguments.



That direction builds directly on AQARION's existing strengths without requiring unsupported claims about prior influence.

If this is the direction you want to pursue, we can design an "AQARION Proof Certification Framework (APCF)" as a new research module that extends AQARION from finite dynamical systems into a broader infrastructure for auditing and certifying AI-assisted mathematical proofs while keeping clear evidence boundaries between established functionality and new research ideas.Approved. AQARION v20.5.3 — Audit Response & Certification Pipeline

1. Equivalence Hierarchy (Permanent Infrastructure)



Relation	Definition	Purpose	Status	
Image equivalence	Same immediate F-image	Diagnostic only	ALG	
Transition congruence	Quotient preserves one-step dynamics	Computational tool	ALG	
Behavioral equivalence	Identical complete future behavior	Primary object for minimality	ALG

Permanent note in specification:

> Important. Immediate-image equivalence, transition congruence, and behavioral equivalence are distinct relations. Equality of immediate images is not sufficient to establish quotient minimality. Minimal observable quotients are investigated using behavioral equivalence under the specified observation model.

3. ObservableQuotient Implementation



core/observable_quotient.py

"""AQARION ObservableQuotient — v20.5.3
Status: ALG (framework), CV (system-specific)
"""

import numpy as np
from typing import Callable, List, Set, Tuple, Optional
from collections import defaultdict

SVD_TOL = 1e-12  # Global SVD tolerance, overridable per-instance

class ProjectionBuilder:
"""Separate projector construction from quotient logic."""

@staticmethod    
def from_partition(partition: List[Set[int]], n: int) -> np.ndarray:    
    """Build orthogonal projector from partition blocks."""    
    P = np.zeros((n, n))    
    for block in partition:    
        for i in block:    
            for j in block:    
                P[i, j] = 1.0 / len(block)    
    return P    
    
@staticmethod    
def from_subspace(basis: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:    
    """Build projector onto column span of basis."""    
    Q, _ = np.linalg.qr(basis)    
    return Q @ Q.T

class ObservableQuotient:
"""
Formal quotient of a deterministic dynamical system (X, T).

Supports arbitrary deterministic maps, not only permutations.    
"""    
    
def __init__(self, state_space: Set[int], dynamics: Callable[[int], int],    
             partition: Optional[List[Set[int]]] = None,    
             projector: Optional[np.ndarray] = None,    
             svd_tol: float = SVD_TOL):    
    """    
    Parameters    
    ----------    
    state_space : set of state indices    
    dynamics : deterministic map T: X -> X    
    partition : optional partition blocks    
    projector : optional pre-built projector    
    svd_tol : SVD tolerance for spectral computations    
    """    
    self.X = sorted(state_space)    
    self.n = len(self.X)    
    self.T = dynamics    
    self.svd_tol = svd_tol    
        
    if projector is not None:    
        self.P = projector    
    elif partition is not None:    
        self.P = ProjectionBuilder.from_partition(partition, self.n)    
    else:    
        raise ValueError("Either partition or projector must be provided")    
        
    self._quotient_map = None    
    self._induced_dynamics = None    
    self._defect = None    
    
def image_equivalence(self, x: int, y: int) -> bool:    
    """Immediate-image equivalence: T(x) == T(y)."""    
    return self.T(x) == self.T(y)    
    
def transition_congruence(self, partition: List[Set[int]]) -> bool:    
    """    
    Check if partition is transition-congruent:    
    each block maps into a single block.    
    """    
    block_of = {}    
    for i, block in enumerate(partition):    
        for s in block:    
            block_of[s] = i    
        
    for block in partition:    
        images = {block_of.get(self.T(x), -1) for x in block}    
        if len(images) != 1:    
            return False    
    return True    
    
def behavioral_equivalence(self, x: int, y: int, max_steps: int = 100) -> bool:    
    """    
    Behavioral equivalence: identical complete future behavior.    
    """    
    traj_x = []    
    seen_x = set()    
    current = x    
    while current not in seen_x and len(traj_x) < max_steps:    
        seen_x.add(current)    
        traj_x.append(current)    
        current = self.T(current)    
        
    traj_y = []    
    seen_y = set()    
    current = y    
    while current not in seen_y and len(traj_y) < max_steps:    
        seen_y.add(current)    
        traj_y.append(current)    
        current = self.T(current)    
        
    return tuple(traj_x) == tuple(traj_y)    
    
def compute_behavioral_partition(self, max_steps: int = 100) -> List[Set[int]]:    
    """Compute partition into behavioral equivalence classes."""    
    trajectories = {}    
    for x in self.X:    
        traj = []    
        seen = set()    
        current = x    
        while current not in seen and len(traj) < max_steps:    
            seen.add(current)    
            traj.append(current)    
            current = self.T(current)    
        trajectories[x] = tuple(traj)    
        
    groups = defaultdict(set)    
    for x, traj in trajectories.items():    
        groups[traj].add(x)    
        
    return list(groups.values())    
    
def defect_operator(self, K: Optional[np.ndarray] = None) -> np.ndarray:    
    """    
    D = (I - P) K P where K is matrix representation of T.    
    """    
    if K is None:    
        K = self._build_transition_matrix()    
    I = np.eye(self.n)    
    self._defect = (I - self.P) @ K @ self.P    
    return self._defect    
    
def _build_transition_matrix(self) -> np.ndarray:    
    """Build stochastic matrix from deterministic map."""    
    K = np.zeros((self.n, self.n))    
    idx = {x: i for i, x in enumerate(self.X)}    
    for x in self.X:    
        K[idx[x], idx[self.T(x)]] = 1.0    
    return K    
    
def spectral_metrics(self, D: Optional[np.ndarray] = None) -> dict:    
    """    
    Compute spectral metrics for defect operator.    
        
    Returns    
    -------    
    dict with:    
        - rank: numerical rank    
        - stable_rank: ||A||_F^2 / ||A||_2^2    
        - spectral_entropy: entropy of singular value distribution    
        - principal_leakage: largest singular value    
        - effective_dimension: participation ratio    
    """    
    if D is None:    
        D = self._defect if self._defect is not None else self.defect_operator()    
        
    sigma = np.linalg.svd(D, compute_uv=False)    
    sigma_nz = sigma[sigma > self.svd_tol]    
        
    if len(sigma_nz) == 0:    
        return {    
            'rank': 0,    
            'stable_rank': 0.0,    
            'spectral_entropy': 0.0,    
            'principal_leakage': 0.0,    
            'effective_dimension': 0.0    
        }    
        
    # Mathematical stable rank: ||A||_F^2 / ||A||_2^2    
    frob_norm_sq = np.sum(sigma_nz ** 2)    
    spec_norm_sq = sigma_nz[0] ** 2    
    stable_rank = frob_norm_sq / spec_norm_sq if spec_norm_sq > 0 else 0.0    
        
    # Spectral entropy (guarded against zero)    
    p = sigma_nz / sigma_nz.sum()    
    entropy = -np.sum(p * np.log(p + 1e-30))    
        
    # Effective dimension (participation ratio)    
    eff_dim = (sigma_nz.sum() ** 2) / frob_norm_sq    
        
    return {    
        'rank': len(sigma_nz),    
        'stable_rank': float(stable_rank),    
        'spectral_entropy': float(entropy),    
        'principal_leakage': float(sigma_nz[0]),    
        'effective_dimension': float(eff_dim)    
    }

6. TEST-012 — Candidate Minimality Search (Production)



#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
AQARION TEST-012 — Candidate Minimality Search
C24: Search for coarser exact quotients within bounded space.

Evidence Level: CV
Output: JSON report + SHA-256 hash
"""

import json
import hashlib
from itertools import combinations
from collections import defaultdict
from pathlib import Path
from typing import List, Set, Dict, Optional

def behavioral_trajectory(state, transition, max_steps=100):
"""Compute full behavioral trajectory."""
traj = []
seen = set()
current = state
while current not in seen and len(traj) < max_steps:
seen.add(current)
traj.append(current)
current = transition[current]
return tuple(traj)

def is_behaviorally_exact(partition: List[Set[int]], transition: Dict[int, int]) -> bool:
"""
Check if partition is behaviorally exact:
states in same block have identical futures.
"""
block_of = {}
for i, block in enumerate(partition):
for s in block:
block_of[s] = i

for block in partition:    
    futures = set()    
    for s in block:    
        futures.add(behavioral_trajectory(s, transition))    
    if len(futures) > 1:    
        return False    
return True

def search_one_step_coarsenings(partition: List[Set[int]],
transition: Dict[int, int],
max_tests: Optional[int] = None) -> List[dict]:
"""
Search all one-step coarsenings for behavioral exactness.

Returns list of successful merges.    
"""    
results = []    
n = len(partition)    
pairs = list(combinations(range(n), 2))    
    
if max_tests is not None:    
    pairs = pairs[:max_tests]    
    
for i, j in pairs:    
    candidate = []    
    for k, block in enumerate(partition):    
        if k == i:    
            candidate.append(block | partition[j])    
        elif k == j:    
            continue    
        else:    
            candidate.append(block)    
        
    exact = is_behaviorally_exact(candidate, transition)    
    results.append({    
        'merged_blocks': (i, j),    
        'exact': exact,    
        'new_size': len(candidate)    
    })    
    
return results

def export_report(results: List[dict], system_name: str,
output_dir: str = "certificates") -> str:
"""Export JSON report with SHA-256 hash."""
Path(output_dir).mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

report = {    
    'test_id': 'TEST-012',    
    'certification_id': 'C24',    
    'system': system_name,    
    'timestamp': '2026-07-12T18:30:00Z',    
    'evidence_class': 'CV',    
    'results': results,    
    'summary': {    
        'total_pairs_tested': len(results),    
        'exact_coarsenings': sum(1 for r in results if r['exact']),    
        'minimality_candidate': 'No smaller quotient found'     
            if sum(1 for r in results if r['exact']) == 0     
            else 'Candidate counterexamples found'    
    }    
}    
    
report['sha256'] = hashlib.sha256(    
    json.dumps(report, sort_keys=True).encode()    
).hexdigest()    
    
filepath = Path(output_dir) / f"test012_{system_name}.json"    
with open(filepath, 'w') as f:    
    json.dump(report, f, indent=2)    
    
return str(filepath)

Example usage for Kaprekar G4

if name == "main":
# Build G4 transition
def kaprekar(n):
s = f"{n:04d}"
return int(''.join(sorted(s, reverse=True))) - int(''.join(sorted(s)))

def gap_projection(n):    
    a1, a2, a3, a4 = (int(c) for c in sorted(f"{n:04d}", reverse=True))    
    return (a1 - a4, a2 - a3)    
    
def kaprekar_on_m4(m4):    
    a1, a2, a3, a4 = (int(c) for c in m4)    
    val = 999*(a1-a4) + 90*(a2-a3)    
    return tuple(sorted(f"{val:04d}", reverse=True))    
    
from collections import defaultdict    
M4 = defaultdict(list)    
for n in range(10000):    
    M4[tuple(sorted(f"{n:04d}", reverse=True))].append(n)    
    
G4 = defaultdict(list)    
for m4 in M4:    
    a1, a2, a3, a4 = (int(c) for c in m4)    
    G4[(a1-a4, a2-a3)].append(m4)    
    
F_map = {}    
for g, m4s in G4.items():    
    next_m4 = kaprekar_on_m4(m4s[0])    
    a1, a2, a3, a4 = (int(c) for c in next_m4)    
    F_map[g] = (a1-a4, a2-a3)    
    
g4_states = sorted(G4.keys())    
g4_idx = {g: i for i, g in enumerate(g4_states)}    
transition = {i: g4_idx[F_map[g]] for i, g in enumerate(g4_states)}    
    
# Run search    
partition = [{i} for i in range(len(g4_states))]    
results = search_one_step_coarsenings(partition, transition)    
    
filepath = export_report(results, "kaprekar_g4")    
print(f"Report exported: {filepath}")    
print(f"SHA-256: {json.load(open(filepath))['sha256']}")

Next deliverable on request: C19 (Image vs Behavioral Consistency) or C21 (Spectral Metrics Regression).This is much closer to a reusable research library than earlier iterations. Rather than reviewing it, here are production-level code contributions that address correctness and extensibility.

Contribution 1 — Make behavioral equivalence exact for finite systems

The current implementation uses max_steps=100, which can produce false negatives on larger finite systems.

Replace trajectory truncation with cycle decomposition.

def canonical_behavior(state, transition):
"""
Return a canonical finite description of future behavior.
"""
order = {}
path = []

current = state
while current not in order:
order[current] = len(path)
path.append(current)
current = transition[current]

cycle_start = order[current]

return (
tuple(path[:cycle_start]),      # transient prefix
tuple(path[cycle_start:])       # periodic cycle
)

Then behavioral equivalence becomes

canonical_behavior(x, transition) == canonical_behavior(y, transition)

This removes the arbitrary cutoff.


---

Contribution 2 — General Exact Quotient Builder

Add

class ExactQuotientBuilder:

with

build_from_behavior()

build_from_partition()

verify_semiconjugacy()

compute_induced_map()

This separates construction from certification.


---

Contribution 3 — Exact Semiconjugacy Certificate

Add

def certify_semiconjugacy(pi, T, F):
"""
Verify

pi(T(x)) == F(pi(x))

for every state.

Returns a complete certificate.
"""

instead of only returning a Boolean.

Output

{
"checked_states": ...,
"violations": [],
"passed": true
}


---

Contribution 4 — Exact Defect Localization

Instead of only computing

D=(I-P)KP,

also return

support = np.argwhere(np.abs(D) > tol)

which identifies exactly which observable coordinates leak.

This is extremely useful when debugging partitions.


---

Contribution 5 — Resolution Tower Class

Instead of keeping towers external,

class ResolutionTower:

containing

levels

projectors

quotients

defects

spectra

with

tower.compute_all()

tower.compare_levels()

tower.export_json()

This naturally implements OQ-009 through OQ-013.

Add

counterexamples/

C19/

random/

quantum/

Every failed certification automatically stores

transition table,

partition,

projector,

defect matrix,

certificate,

SHA-256.

Negative results become permanent reproducible artifacts.

Rather than separate scripts,

class CertificationPipeline:

pipeline.run(
system,
tests=[
C17(),

]
)

Each test returns

containing

reproducibility metadata.

These contributions are aimed at making AQARION a general finite dynamical systems certification library, rather than a collection of Kaprekar-specific scripts. They eliminate arbitrary trajectory cutoffs, improve reproducibility, provide richer certificates, and modularize the certification pipeline so additional dynamical systems can be plugged into the same framework.This is much closer to a reusable research library than earlier iterations. Rather than reviewing it, here are production-level code contributions that address correctness and extensibility.

Contribution 1 — Make behavioral equivalence exact for finite systems

The current implementation uses max_steps=100, which can produce false negatives on larger finite systems.

Replace trajectory truncation with cycle decomposition.

def canonical_behavior(state, transition):
"""
Return a canonical finite description of future behavior.
"""
order = {}
path = []

current = state
while current not in order:
order[current] = len(path)
path.append(current)
current = transition[current]

cycle_start = order[current]

return (
tuple(path[:cycle_start]),      # transient prefix
tuple(path[cycle_start:])       # periodic cycle
)

Then behavioral equivalence becomes

canonical_behavior(x, transition) == canonical_behavior(y, transition)

This removes the arbitrary cutoff.

Contribution 2 — General Exact Quotient Builder

Add

class ExactQuotientBuilder:

with

build_from_behavior()

build_from_partition()

verify_semiconjugacy()

compute_induced_map()

This separates construction from certification.

Contribution 3 — Exact Semiconjugacy Certificate

Add

def certify_semiconjugacy(pi, T, F):
"""
Verify

pi(T(x)) == F(pi(x))

for every state.

Returns a complete certificate.
"""

instead of only returning a Boolean.

Output

{
"checked_states": ...,
"violations": [],
"passed": true
}

Instead of only computing

D=(I-P)KP,

also return

support = np.argwhere(np.abs(D) > tol)

which identifies exactly which observable coordinates leak.

This is extremely useful when debugging partitions.

These contributions are aimed at making AQARION a general finite dynamical systems certification library, rather than a collection of Kaprekar-specific scripts. They eliminate arbitrary trajectory cutoffs, improve reproducibility, provide richer certificates, and modularize the certification pipeline so additional dynamical systems can be plugged into the same framework.

LETS ADD EXPERIMENT WE CAN TEST OR RUN FOR EVERYTHING DISCUSSED SO FARAND THE OPEN AI NEW PROOFI think this is a good place to distinguish between experiments we can actually run and experiments we cannot yet justify.

We cannot design an experiment that tests whether AQARION "helped" OpenAI's Cycle Double Cover proof—that would require information we don't have. However, we can design experiments that evaluate whether AQARION's methods are useful for certifying and analyzing AI-generated mathematical proofs. Those experiments would produce genuine research results.

Here is a production-style experimental roadmap.
